name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
أَلَآ إِنَّهُمْ يَثْنُونَ صُدُورَهُمْ لِيَسْتَخْفُوا۟ مِنْهُ ۚ أَلَا حِينَ يَسْتَغْشُونَ ثِيَابَهُمْ يَعْلَمُ مَا يُسِرُّونَ وَمَا يُعْلِنُونَ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ (٥)
```

```english
Look! They fold up their breasts to hide [their secret feelings] from Him. Look! When they draw their cloaks over their heads, He knows whatever they keep secret and whatever they disclose. Indeed, He knows best whatever is in the breasts. (5)
```

```arabic
۞ وَمَا مِن دَآبَّةٍ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ إِلَّا عَلَى ٱللَّهِ رِزْقُهَا وَيَعْلَمُ مُسْتَقَرَّهَا وَمُسْتَوْدَعَهَا ۚ كُلٌّ فِى كِتَـٰبٍ مُّبِينٍ (٦)
```

```english
There is no animal on the earth, but that its sustenance lies with Allah, and He knows its [enduring] abode and its temporary place of lodging. Everything is in a manifest Book. (6)
```

```arabic
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ فِى سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ وَكَانَ عَرْشُهُۥ عَلَى ٱلْمَآءِ لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلًا ۗ وَلَئِن قُلْتَ إِنَّكُم مَّبْعُوثُونَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ٱلْمَوْتِ لَيَقُولَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا سِحْرٌ مُّبِينٌ (٧)
```

```english
It is He who created the heavens and the earth in six days—and His Throne was [then] upon the waters—that He may test you [to see] which of you is best in conduct. Yet if you say, ‘You will indeed be raised up after death,’ the faithless will surely say, ‘This is nothing but plain magic.’ (7)
```

```arabic
وَلَئِنْ أَخَّرْنَا عَنْهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابَ إِلَىٰٓ أُمَّةٍ مَّعْدُودَةٍ لَّيَقُولُنَّ مَا يَحْبِسُهُۥٓ ۗ أَلَا يَوْمَ يَأْتِيهِمْ لَيْسَ مَصْرُوفًا عَنْهُمْ وَحَاقَ بِهِم مَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (٨)
```

```english
And if We defer their punishment until a certain time, they will surely say, ‘What holds it back?’ Look! On the day it overtakes them it shall not be turned away from them, and they will be besieged by what they used to deride. (8)
```

```arabic
وَلَئِنْ أَذَقْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ مِنَّا رَحْمَةً ثُمَّ نَزَعْنَـٰهَا مِنْهُ إِنَّهُۥ لَيَـُٔوسٌ كَفُورٌ (٩)
```

```english
If We let man taste a mercy from Us, and then withdraw it from him, he becomes despondent, ungrateful. (9)
```

```arabic
وَلَئِنْ أَذَقْنَـٰهُ نَعْمَآءَ بَعْدَ ضَرَّآءَ مَسَّتْهُ لَيَقُولَنَّ ذَهَبَ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتُ عَنِّىٓ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ لَفَرِحٌ فَخُورٌ (١٠)
```

```english
And if We let him have a taste of Our blessings after adversities have befallen him, he will surely say, ‘All ills have left me.’ Indeed, he becomes a vain braggart, (10)
```

```arabic
إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ كَبِيرٌ (١١)
```

```english
excepting those who are patient and do righteous deeds. For such there will be forgiveness and a great reward. (11)
```

```arabic
فَلَعَلَّكَ تَارِكٌۢ بَعْضَ مَا يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَيْكَ وَضَآئِقٌۢ بِهِۦ صَدْرُكَ أَن يَقُولُوا۟ لَوْلَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَيْهِ كَنزٌ أَوْ جَآءَ مَعَهُۥ مَلَكٌ ۚ إِنَّمَآ أَنتَ نَذِيرٌ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ وَكِيلٌ (١٢)
```

```english
[Look out] lest you should disregard aught of what has been revealed to you, and be upset because they say, ‘Why has not a treasure been sent down to him, or [why does] not an angel accompany him?’ You are only a warner, and Allah watches over all things. (12)
```

```arabic
أَمْ يَقُولُونَ ٱفْتَرَىٰهُ ۖ قُلْ فَأْتُوا۟ بِعَشْرِ سُوَرٍ مِّثْلِهِۦ مُفْتَرَيَـٰتٍ وَٱدْعُوا۟ مَنِ ٱسْتَطَعْتُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (١٣)
```

```english
Do they say, ‘He has fabricated it?’ Say, ‘Then bring ten surahs like it, fabricated, and invoke whomever you can, besides Allah, should you be truthful.’ (13)
```

```arabic
فَإِلَّمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا۟ لَكُمْ فَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّمَآ أُنزِلَ بِعِلْمِ ٱللَّهِ وَأَن لَّآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ فَهَلْ أَنتُم مُّسْلِمُونَ (١٤)
```

```english
But if they do not respond to you, know that it has been sent down by Allah’s knowledge, and that there is no god except Him. Will you, then, submit [to Allah]? (14)
```

```arabic
مَن كَانَ يُرِيدُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَزِينَتَهَا نُوَفِّ إِلَيْهِمْ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ فِيهَا وَهُمْ فِيهَا لَا يُبْخَسُونَ (١٥)
```

```english
As for those who desire the life of this world and its glitter, We will recompense them fully for their works therein, and they shall not be underpaid in it. (15)
```

```arabic
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَيْسَ لَهُمْ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ إِلَّا ٱلنَّارُ ۖ وَحَبِطَ مَا صَنَعُوا۟ فِيهَا وَبَـٰطِلٌ مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (١٦)
```

```english
They are the ones for whom there shall be nothing in the Hereafter but Fire: what they had accomplished in the world has failed, and their works have come to naught. (16)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses present segments and chapters concerning the actions and claims of the polytheists in their rejection of the Prophethood of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the Book revealed to him. They respond to these claims by establishing proof, such as their attempt to hide from Allah, their saying: "What withholds the punishment from us?", their saying: "Why has a treasure not been sent down to him, or an angel come with him?", and their claim that he fabricated the Quran. The verses also contain other divine insights.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «أَلا إِنَّهُمْ يَثْنُونَ صُدُورَهُمْ لِيَسْتَخْفُوا مِنْهُ» (Behold! They fold up their breasts to hide from Him) until the end of the verse. To **fold** (ثنى) something means to bend it, fold it, or turn one part over another. It is said in *al-Majma‘*: "The root of **folding** (الثني) is bending. You say: 'I folded him away from such-and-such,' meaning I turned him away. From this comes the word 'two' (الاثنان) because one is 'folded' or joined to the other in meaning. From it also comes 'praise' (الثناء) because it involves 'folding' or repeating virtues in commendation. And from it comes 'exception' (الاستثناء) because it is 'folded' back by being excluded from the whole." He also said: "**Seeking concealment** (الاستخفاء) is the attempt to hide something. It is said: *istakhfa* and *takhaffa* with the same meaning, just as *istaghsha* and *taghashsha* (to cover oneself) are used."

Thus, the meaning of His saying: «يَثْنُونَ صُدُورَهُمْ لِيَسْتَخْفُوا مِنْهُ» is that they lean their chests backward and lower their heads to hide from the Book—that is, from listening to it when it is recited. This is an allusion (كناية) to their hiding from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and those present with him during the recitation of the Quran for the purpose of conveyance (التبليغ), so they would not be seen there and thus have the proof (الحجة) established against them.

And His saying: «أَلا حِينَ يَسْتَغْشُونَ ثِيابَهُمْ يَعْلَمُ» (Behold! When they draw their garments over themselves, He knows...) etc. It is as if they were also covering their heads with their garments while hiding by folding their chests. So Allah, the Glorified, mentioned this and informed them that He, the Exalted, knows at that moment what they conceal and what they reveal. Thus, hiding does not avail them in avoiding the hearing of the Quran, for Allah knows their secrets and their public affairs.

It has also been said: The meaning of their drawing their garments over themselves refers to their covering themselves in their homes at night when taking to their beds for sleep—which is the most hidden state for a human and his most private condition. The meaning would then be: they fold their chests to hide from this Book when it is recited to them, yet Allah knows their secrets and their public affairs even in their most hidden state, which is the state of covering themselves with their garments for sleep. This interpretation is not without merit.

This is what the context (السياق) provides regarding the meaning of the verse. Other meanings far from the context have been mentioned, such as the claim that the pronoun in «لِيَسْتَخْفُوا مِنْهُ» (to hide from him/it) refers to Allah, the Exalted, or to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). Some said: «يَثْنُونَ صُدُورَهُمْ» means they fold their hearts upon disbelief (الكفر), and others said: they fold them upon enmity toward the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). All such mentioned meanings are far-fetched.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَما مِنْ دَابَّةٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ إِلَّا عَلَى اللهِ رِزْقُها» (There is no animal on the earth, but that its provision is due from Allah) until the end of the verse. An **animal** (الدابة), according to linguistic lexicons, is anything that creeps or moves (يدب ويتحرك). It is frequently used for a specific type, but the context necessitates the general meaning, as the discourse is aimed at demonstrating the vastness of His knowledge, the Exalted. For this reason, it follows His saying: «أَلا حِينَ يَسْتَغْشُونَ ثِيابَهُمْ يَعْلَمُ ما يُسِرُّونَ وَما يُعْلِنُونَ إِنَّهُ عَلِيمٌ بِذاتِ الصُّدُورِ» (Behold! When they draw their garments over themselves, He knows what they conceal and what they reveal. Indeed, He knows best what is in the breasts).

This meaning—namely, that the mention of the necessity of providing for every animal is to demonstrate the vastness of His knowledge of every animal in all its states—requires that His saying: «وَيَعْلَمُ مُسْتَقَرَّها وَمُسْتَوْدَعَها» (and He knows its settling place and its repository) be in the position of an explanatory conjunction (عطف التفسير) for His saying: «عَلَى اللهِ رِزْقُها» (its provision is due from Allah). Thus, the meaning returns to the fact that every animal on earth is guaranteed provision by Allah—it shall never remain without provision—for He, the Exalted, is All-Knowing of it and aware of its state wherever it may be. If it is in a **settling place** (مستقر) from which it does not emerge, like a fish in water or a shellfish in the spot where it has settled in the earth, He provides for it there. And if it is outside its settling place and is in a **repository** (مستودع) which it will soon leave for its settling place, like a bird in the air, or a traveler far from home, or a fetus in the womb, He provides for it there. In short, He, the Exalted, is All-Knowing of the state of every animal on earth; and how could He not be, when its provision is His responsibility? Provision (الرزق) only reaches the provided-for (المرزوق) through the knowledge of the Provider (الرازق) regarding the provided-for and His awareness of where it resides, whether in a permanent or temporary place, a settling place or a repository.

From this, it becomes clear that the **settling place** (المستقر) and the **repository** (المستودع) refer to the place where the animal settles as long as it is a creature creeping upon the earth living its worldly life, and the place where it resides then leaves and departs from. As for what some commentators have mentioned—that the settling place and repository refer to their places in life and after death, or that they refer to the loins and the wombs, or that they refer to their dwellings on earth when they exist in actuality and their repositories in matter and potentiality before existence—these are meanings far from the context of the verse, unless one considers His saying: «وَيَعْلَمُ مُسْتَقَرَّها وَمُسْتَوْدَعَها» as a completely new sentence, not explanatory of what preceded it.

A discussion suitable for this context has already been presented regarding His saying: «وَهُوَ الَّذِي أَنْشَأَكُمْ مِنْ نَفْسٍ واحِدَةٍ فَمُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمُسْتَوْدَعٌ» (It is He who created you from a single soul, then [there is] a settling place and a repository) [Al-An'am: 98]; let whoever wishes refer back to it.

As for His saying: «عَلَى اللهِ رِزْقُها» (its provision is due from Allah), it indicates the necessity of provision upon Him, the Exalted. It has been repeated in the Quran that provision (الرزق) is among His acts exclusive to Him, and that it is a right (حق) for the creation upon Him, the Exalted. He, the Exalted, said: «أَمَّنْ هذَا الَّذِي يَرْزُقُكُمْ إِنْ أَمْسَكَ رِزْقَهُ» (Who is it that may provide for you if He withholds His provision?) [Al-Mulk: 21], and He said: «إِنَّ اللهَ هُوَ الرَّزَّاقُ ذُو الْقُوَّةِ الْمَتِينُ» (Indeed it is Allah who is the All-provider, the Possessor of all might, the Unshakable) [Adh-Dhariyat: 58], and He said: «وَفِي السَّماءِ رِزْقُكُمْ وَما تُوعَدُونَ فَوَ رَبِّ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ إِنَّهُ لَحَقٌّ مِثْلَ ما أَنَّكُمْ تَنْطِقُونَ» (And in the heaven is your provision and what you are promised. By the Lord of the heaven and the earth, it is indeed as real as your speaking) [Adh-Dhariyat: 22-23].

There is no harm in establishing a right for another upon Him, the Exalted, if He, the Exalted, is the one who establishes and necessitates that upon Himself without any external interference. There are parallels to this in His speech, such as His saying: «كَتَبَ عَلى نَفْسِهِ الرَّحْمَةَ» (He has made mercy incumbent upon Himself) [Al-An'am: 12], and His saying: «وَكانَ حَقًّا عَلَيْنا نَصْرُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ» (and it was a must for Us to help the believers) [Ar-Rum: 47], among other verses.

Rational consideration (الاعتبار العقلي) supports this, for provision is that by which a living creature sustains its existence. Since its existence is from the effusion of His bounty (فيض جوده), the provision upon which it depends must also be from Him. Since He, the Exalted, has no partner in its creation, He has no partner in that upon which its existence depends, such as provision.

Some discussion regarding the meaning of the **Manifest Book** (الكتاب المبين) — *the divine record that encompasses all things and events in the universe* — has already passed in Surah al-An'am, verse 59, and in Surah Yunus, verse 61; so let it be referred to.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَهُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ فِي سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ وَكانَ عَرْشُهُ عَلَى الْماءِ» (It is He who created the heavens and the earth in six days, and His Throne was upon the water). A comprehensive discourse on the description of the creation of the heavens and the earth, as apparent from His speech and explained by what has been narrated from the People of the House (أهل العصمة) (peace be upon them), is deferred to the upcoming commentary on Surah Ha Mim as-Sajdah, Allah willing.

The summary of the discourse that clarifies the meaning of His saying: «سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ» (six days) and His saying: «وَكانَ عَرْشُهُ عَلَى الْماءِ» (and His Throne was upon the water) is that it appears that what He, the Exalted, mentions as "the heavens" (السماوات)—in the plural—and pairs with "the earth," describing their creation in six days, are levels of the witnessed physical creation (الخلق الجسماني المشهود) that rise above our earth. For everything that is above you and shades you is a "heaven" (سماء), as has been said, and "high" and "low" are relative terms (معاني إضافية).

They are levels of the witnessed physical creation that rise above our earth and encompass it, for the earth is spherical in shape, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: «يُغْشِي اللَّيْلَ النَّهارَ يَطْلُبُهُ حَثِيثاً» (He draws the night's cover over the day, which pursues it incessantly) [Al-A'raf: 54].

The first heaven (السماء الأولى) is that which is adorned by the lamps of the stars and planets; it is the level that contains them, or it is above them and adorned by them, like a ceiling adorned with lamps and niches. As for what is above the lowest heaven, nothing of its description has appeared in His speech except what is in His saying, the Exalted: «سَبْعَ سَماواتٍ طِباقاً» (seven heavens in layers) [Al-Mulk: 3], and His saying: «أَلَمْ تَرَوْا كَيْفَ خَلَقَ اللهُ سَبْعَ سَماواتٍ طِباقاً وَجَعَلَ الْقَمَرَ فِيهِنَّ نُوراً وَجَعَلَ الشَّمْسَ سِراجاً» (Have you not regarded how Allah created the seven heavens in layers, and made the moon a light in them, and made the sun a lamp?) [Nuh: 15-16], which indicates the correspondence (مطابقة) of one to another.

Allah, the Glorified, mentioned in the description of their creation that they were joined (رتقاء), then He clove them asunder (فتقها); they were scattered and dissipated, then He gathered and heaped them; and they were smoke (دخان), then He made them heavens. He, the Exalted, said: «أَوَلَمْ يَرَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَنَّ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ كانَتا رَتْقاً فَفَتَقْناهُما وَجَعَلْنا مِنَ الْماءِ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ حَيٍّ أَفَلا يُؤْمِنُونَ» (Have the faithless not regarded that the heavens and the earth were a solid mass, then We clove them asunder, and We made every living thing out of water? Will they not then have faith?) [Al-Anbiya: 30]. And He said: «ثُمَّ اسْتَوى إِلَى السَّماءِ وَهِيَ دُخانٌ فَقالَ لَها وَلِلْأَرْضِ ائْتِيا طَوْعاً أَوْ كَرْهاً قالَتا أَتَيْنا طائِعِينَ فَقَضاهُنَّ سَبْعَ سَماواتٍ فِي يَوْمَيْنِ وَأَوْحى فِي كُلِّ سَماءٍ أَمْرَها» (Then He turned to the heaven, and it was smoke, and He said to it and to the earth, ‘Come! willingly or unwillingly.’ They said, ‘We come willingly.’ So He completed them as seven heavens in two days, and revealed to each heaven its order) [Fussilat: 11-12]. This informs us that the creation of the heavens was completed in two days. A "day" (اليوم) is a significant period of time (برهة من الزمان), and it is not necessary for a "day" in every context and vessel to correspond to the day of our earth resulting from a single rotation of its axial motion, just as one day on the moon of this earth equals approximately twenty-nine and a half earth days. The use of "day" for a period of time is common in speech.

Thus, Allah, the Glorified, created the seven heavens in two periods of time, just as He said regarding the earth: «خَلَقَ الْأَرْضَ فِي يَوْمَيْنِ» (created the earth in two days) until He said: «وَقَدَّرَ فِيها أَقْواتَها فِي أَرْبَعَةِ أَيَّامٍ» (and ordained its nourishments in four days) [Fussilat: 9-10]. He informed of its creation in two days—which are two eras or stages—and the placing of nourishments in four days, which are the four seasons.

What is gathered from the verses is, firstly: that the creation of the heavens and the earth in their current description and form was not from absolute non-existence (عدم بحت); rather, they were preceded in existence by a uniform, heaped, and gathered matter. Then some of its parts were separated from others, and it was made into an earth in two periods of time, while the heaven was smoke, so it was separated and decreed as seven heavens in two periods of time.

Secondly: that the living things we see were made from water; thus, the substance of water is the substance of life.

From what we have presented, the meaning of the verse at hand becomes clear. His saying: «هُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ فِي سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ» (It is He who created the heavens and the earth in six days)—the meaning of their creation is the gathering of their parts, their separation, and their claving asunder from the rest of the uniform, heaped matter mixed with them. The primary creation and joining (الرتق) were completed in the heavens in two days and in the earth in two days, leaving two days of the six for other matters.

As for His saying: «وَكانَ عَرْشُهُ عَلَى الْماءِ» (and His Throne was upon the water), it is a circumstantial clause (حال). The meaning is: His Throne, on the day He created them, was upon the water. The Throne being upon the water at that time is an allusion (كناية) to the fact that His dominion (ملك), the Exalted, was established at that time upon this water, which is the substance of life. For the Throne of the King (عرش الملك) is the manifestation of His dominion, and its being established upon a place is the establishment of His dominion over it, just as His mounting (استواء) the Throne is His encompassing the dominion and taking up its governance (تدبير).

The claim of some that the **Throne** (العرش) means "building," taking from His saying, the Exalted: «مِمَّا يَعْرِشُونَ» (of what they build) [An-Nahl: 68], is a discourse far from correct understanding.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلاً» (that He may test you [to see] which of you is best in conduct). The "lam" is for purpose (للغاية), and **testing** (البلاء) is trial and examination. His saying: «أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلاً» (which of you is best in conduct) is an explanation of the trial and examination in the form of an interrogation. The meaning is that He, the Exalted, created the heavens and the earth as He did for the purpose of testing you and distinguishing the righteous among you from the wicked.

It is well known that trial and examination are intended for something else—namely, the distinguishing of the good from the bad and the beautiful from the ugly. Similarly, the good deed and the evil deed are only intended to be distinguished for the sake of the recompense (الجزاء) that follows them. Likewise, the recompense is only intended for the sake of fulfilling the true promise. Therefore, we find Him, the Exalted, mentioning each of these sequential matters as a purpose for creation. Regarding trial being the purpose of creation, He said: «إِنَّا جَعَلْنا ما عَلَى الْأَرْضِ زِينَةً لَها لِنَبْلُوَهُمْ أَيُّهُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلاً» (Indeed We have made whatever is on the earth an adornment for it that We may test them [to see] which of them is best in conduct) [Al-Kahf: 7]. Regarding the meaning of distinguishing and purging, He said: «لِيَمِيزَ اللهُ الْخَبِيثَ مِنَ الطَّيِّبِ» (so that Allah may separate the bad from the good) [Al-Anfal: 37].

Regarding recompense specifically, He said: «وَخَلَقَ اللهُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ بِالْحَقِّ وَلِتُجْزى كُلُّ نَفْسٍ بِما كَسَبَتْ وَهُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ» (Allah created the heavens and the earth with the truth, so that every soul may be requited for what it has earned, and they will not be wronged) [Al-Jathiyah: 22]. Regarding the return being for the fulfillment of the promise, He said: «كَما بَدَأْنا أَوَّلَ خَلْقٍ نُعِيدُهُ وَعْداً عَلَيْنا إِنَّا كُنَّا فاعِلِينَ» (As We originated the first creation, We will bring it back—a promise [binding] on Us; We will indeed do it) [Al-Anbiya: 104]. And regarding worship (العبادة) being the objective in the creation of the two weighty ones (الثقلين), He said: «وَما خَلَقْتُ الْجِنَّ وَالْإِنْسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ» (I did not create the jinn and the humans except that they may worship Me) [Adh-Dhariyat: 56].

Considering righteous deeds or the virtuous human as the purpose of creation does not contradict the creation's inclusion of other purposes, as long as the human is truly one of those purposes. This is because the unity and connection (الاتصال) governing the world make it correct for each species of being to be a purpose for creation, insofar as it is the product of the relationship and the result of the general coupling between its parts. Thus, it is permissible to address each species of creation as being the intended objective of the creation of the heavens and the earth, insofar as they lead to it.

Furthermore, the human is the most perfect and most masterfully crafted of the physical creatures among the heavens, the earth, and what is in them. If he grows well in the aspect of knowledge and action, he becomes superior in essence (ذاتا) to all else, higher in station, and loftier in degree, even if some creations like the heaven are "more intense in creation" than him, as Allah mentioned. It is well known that the perfection of a craft is its intended goal when it includes the imperfect. Therefore, we consider the various stages of human existence—the seminal, the fetal, the childhood, and others—as a prelude to the existence of the sound, perfect human, and so on.

By this explanation, it becomes clear that the best of human individuals—if there is among them one who is absolutely the best—is the purpose for the creation of the heavens and the earth. The wording of the verse also contains an indication or proof of this, for His saying: «أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلاً» (which of you is best in conduct) implies that the intent is to distinguish the one whose conduct is better than others, whether those others are righteous or wicked. Whoever's conduct is better than all other individuals—whether they are righteous but their deeds are below his, or they are wicked—his distinction from them is the intended purpose of creation. By this, what has been narrated in the *Hadith Qudsi* is validated, in His address, the Exalted, to His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): "Were it not for you, I would not have created the celestial spheres," for he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is the best of creation.

In *al-Majma‘*: "Al-Jubba'i said: 'The verse contains proof that there was life before the creation of the heavens, the earth, and the angels, because the creation of the Throne upon the water has no reason for its goodness unless there is in it a grace (لطف) for a legally responsible person (مكلف) who can use it as a proof; thus, there must be a living, responsible person at that time.' Ali ibn Isa said: 'It is not impossible that in informing of that there is a benefit for the responsible ones, so what al-Jubba'i said is not necessary.' This is what al-Murtada (may Allah sanctify his soul) chose." End quote.

**I say:** What they both mentioned is based on the Mu'tazilite view: that the acts of Allah, the Glorified, are caused by objectives (الأغراض) and follow benefits (المصالح) and aspects of goodness (الحسن). Even if this were by creating a creation to inform the responsible ones of it so they might take heed and believe in Him, thereby fulfilling one of their benefits. It has already passed in our previous discussions that Allah, the Glorified, is not ruled over nor influenced by anything else, whether that other thing is a benefit or any other hypothesized thing. Anything else hypothesized is His creation, governed by His command, if it is something with reality and existence. "Judgment belongs only to Allah," and "Allah is the Creator of all things."

Thus, the aspects of goodness and benefit—which are what rule over us and drive us toward our actions—are matters external to our actions, influencing us because we are agents seeking thereby the happiness of life. As for Him, the Glorified, He is far above that. This is because these aspects of goodness and benefit are merely general laws derived from the system of the universe and the relationships circulating between the parts of creation. It is necessary that the universe and the system running within it are His act, and it is quite impossible for a concept abstracted (المفهوم المنتزع) to precede that from which it was abstracted—the act—then bypass it and not be satisfied until it precedes its Agent who brought it into existence.

As for the causal explanation (التعليل) in the verse for the creation of the heavens and the earth by His saying: «لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلاً» (that He may test you [to see] which of you is best in conduct) and its many parallels in the Quran, it and its likes are of the category of causal explanation by the resulting benefits and the branching interests. Allah has informed that His act is not devoid of goodness, as He said: «الَّذِي أَحْسَنَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقَهُ» (who perfected everything He created) [As-Sajdah: 7]. He, the Glorified, is the Good in Whom there is no evil, and He is the Beautiful with Whom there is no ugliness; and what is such can never produce evil or ugliness at all.

The implication of what has passed is not that the meaning of "goodness" (الحسن) is simply whatever is issued by Him, the Exalted, or what He has commanded even if the intellect finds it ugly; nor that the meaning of "ugliness" (القبيح) is what is not issued by Him or what He has forbidden even if the intellect finds it good and approves of it. For such a view is rejected by verses like His saying, the Exalted: «قُلْ إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَأْمُرُ بِالْفَحْشاءِ» (Say, ‘Indeed Allah does not enjoin indecency’) [Al-A'raf: 28].

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَلَئِنْ قُلْتَ إِنَّكُمْ مَبْعُوثُونَ مِنْ بَعْدِ الْمَوْتِ لَيَقُولَنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِنْ هذا إِلَّا سِحْرٌ مُبِينٌ» (And if you say, ‘You will indeed be raised after death,’ the faithless will surely say, ‘This is nothing but plain magic’). Since His saying: «لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ» (that He may test you) etc., points to the Return (المعاد) — *the resurrection and the final gathering of all souls for judgment* — He pointed to what the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to face from the disbelievers when he mentioned the Return, as they would dismiss it as "magic" in speech.

The apparent meaning of the verse is that just as they used to call the wording of the Noble Quran "magic" due to its eloquence and the beauty of its composition, they also used to call the realities of knowledge (حقائق المعارف) that the Quran or the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) informed of—which their minds did not accept, such as the resurrection after death—"magic." Based on this, it is part of their exaggeration in fabricating lies against the Book of Allah and their obstinacy and enmity toward the explicit truth, as they went beyond labeling the wording as magic for its eloquence to labeling the meaning as magic for its correctness and uprightness.

It is possible that "magic" (السحر) here means sophistry and deception by presenting falsehood in the form of truth, by way of mentioning the necessary (الملزوم) and intending the consequence (اللازم). However, this does not align well with the apparent meaning of His saying, the Exalted, in a similar instance: «قُلْ مَنْ بِيَدِهِ مَلَكُوتُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ وَهُوَ يُجِيرُ وَلا يُجارُ عَلَيْهِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ سَيَقُولُونَ لِلَّهِ قُلْ فَأَنَّى تُسْحَرُونَ» (Say, ‘In whose hand is the dominion of all things, and who shelters, and against whom no shelter can be given, if you know?’ They will say, ‘Allah’s.’ Say, ‘Then how are you bewitched?’) [Al-Mu'minun: 88-89].

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَلَئِنْ أَخَّرْنا عَنْهُمُ الْعَذابَ إِلى أُمَّةٍ مَعْدُودَةٍ لَيَقُولُنَّ ما يَحْبِسُهُ» (And if We defer the punishment from them until a numbered time, they will surely say, ‘What withholds it?’) until the end of the verse. The "lam" at the beginning of the verse is for an oath (للقسم); therefore, the response—namely His saying: «لَيَقُولُنَ» (they will surely say)—is emphasized with the "lam" and the "nun." The meaning is: I swear, if We defer from these disbelievers the punishment they deserve, they would say mockingly: "What is it that withholds this promised punishment from us, and why does it not descend upon us and befall us?"

In this, there is an indication or proof that they heard from His speech, the Exalted, or from the speech of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), that which promised them an unavoidable punishment, and that Allah deferred it as a mercy to them, so they mocked it and ridiculed it by saying: «ما يَحْبِسُهُ» (What withholds it?). This is supported by His saying, the Exalted, immediately following: «أَلا يَوْمَ يَأْتِيهِمْ لَيْسَ مَصْرُوفاً عَنْهُمْ» (Behold! On the day it comes to them, it shall not be turned away from them) etc.

By this, it is supported that the Surah—Surah Hud—was revealed after Surah Yunus, because of His saying, the Exalted, therein: «وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَسُولٌ فَإِذا جاءَ رَسُولُهمْ قُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ بِالْقِسْطِ» (There is a messenger for every nation: so when their messenger comes, judgment is made between them with justice) [Yunus: 47] until the end of the verses.

And His saying: «إِلى أُمَّةٍ مَعْدُودَةٍ» (until a numbered time). The **time** (الأمة) here means a period or a moment, as in His saying, the Exalted: «وَقالَ الَّذِي نَجا مِنْهُما وَادَّكَرَ بَعْدَ أُمَّةٍ» (And the one of the two who had been delivered said, remembering after a time...) [Yunus: 45], meaning after a period and a time.

It might also be possible that **nation** (الأمة) refers to a group of people. For Allah, the Glorified, promised to support this religion with a righteous people who do not prefer anything over His religion, and at that time, the believers will be established in their religion which He has approved for them. He said: «فَسَوْفَ يَأْتِي اللهُ بِقَوْمٍ يُحِبُّهُمْ وَيُحِبُّونَهُ أَذِلَّةٍ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَعِزَّةٍ عَلَى الْكافِرِينَ يُجاهِدُونَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَلا يَخافُونَ لَوْمَةَ لائِمٍ» (Allah will soon bring a people whom He loves and who love Him, [who will be] humble towards the believers, mighty against the faithless, waging jihad in the way of Allah, not fearing the blame of any blamer) [Al-Ma'idah: 54]. And He said: «وَعَدَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْكُمْ وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ لَيَسْتَخْلِفَنَّهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ كَمَا اسْتَخْلَفَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ وَلَيُمَكِّنَنَّ لَهُمْ دِينَهُمُ الَّذِي ارْتَضى لَهُمْ... يَعْبُدُونَنِي لا يُشْرِكُونَ بِي شَيْئاً» (Allah has promised those of you who have faith and do righteous deeds that He will surely make them successors in the earth, even as He made those who were before them successors, and He will surely establish for them their religion which He has approved for them... They worship Me, not ascribing any partners to Me) [An-Nur: 55]. This is a plausible interpretation.

It has also been said: The "nation" (الأمة) refers to a group, and they are a people whom Allah will bring after these, who will persist in disbelief, so He will punish them with the punishment of extirpation (عذاب الاستئصال) as He did with the people of Noah. Or they are a people who come after these and persist in disobedience to Allah, so the Resurrection will occur upon them.

Both these views are weak because they are built on the premise that those punished are other than these mocking disbelievers, whereas the apparent meaning of His saying, the Exalted: «أَلا يَوْمَ يَأْتِيهِمْ» (Behold! On the day it comes to them) etc., is that the punished ones are the very ones who mocked by saying: «ما يَحْبِسُهُ» (What withholds it?).

And His saying: «أَلا يَوْمَ يَأْتِيهِمْ لَيْسَ مَصْرُوفاً عَنْهُمْ وَحاقَ بِهِمْ ما كانُوا بِهِ يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ» (Behold! On the day it comes to them, it shall not be turned away from them, and they will be besieged by what they used to mock) is in the position of a response to their saying: «ما يَحْبِسُهُ» (What withholds it?), which was said in mockery. For it is in the sense of rejecting the punishment they were promised. Its summary is: "This punishment that threatens us, if it were true, there would be no reason for its withholding, for we are disbelievers who do not turn away from disbelief nor leave it. Thus, the delay in the descent of punishment without a cause for delay—rather with a cause for its acceleration—reveals that it is of the nature of a false promise."

So Allah responded to that by stating that it will surely come to them, and no turner will turn it away from them on that day, and this punishment which they used to mock will surround them.

From what has passed, it becomes clear that this punishment they are threatened with is a worldly punishment (عذاب دنيوي) that will surround them and descend upon them, distinct from the punishment of the Hereafter. Accordingly, this verse and the one before it each mention something of what the disbelievers raved about in their ignorance. The previous verse mentions that when the Resurrection is mentioned to them and they are warned of the punishment of the Day of Judgment, they say: "This is nothing but plain magic." This verse mentions that when Allah defers the punishment from them until a certain time and they are informed of this, they say mockingly: "What withholds it?"

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَلَئِنْ أَذَقْنَا الْإِنْسانَ مِنَّا رَحْمَةً ثُمَّ نَزَعْناها مِنْهُ إِنَّهُ لَيَؤُسٌ كَفُورٌ» (If We let man taste a mercy from Us, and then withdraw it from him, he is indeed despondent and ungrateful). It is said in *al-Majma‘*: "**Tasting** (الذوق) is taking something with the mouth to perceive the flavor. Allah, the Glorified, called the arrival of pleasures to man 'tasting' because of the speed of their disappearance, likening them to what is tasted then vanishes, as it is said: 'dreams of sleep or like a vanishing shadow.' **Withdrawal** (النزع) is uprooting a thing from its place. **Despondent** (اليئوس) is the intensive form (فعول) of *ya'isa* (to despair). **Despair** (اليأس) is the certainty that the expected thing will not happen, and its opposite is hope." End quote.

"Mercy" (الرحمة) has been placed in the verse in the position of "blessing" (النعمة) to indicate that the blessings Allah gives to man are characterized as mercy—which is the lifting of man's need in what he requires without entitlement or obligation. The meaning is: if We give man some of the blessings he enjoys then withdraw them, he despairs of them, and his despair becomes intense as if he does not see their return to him a second time as possible, and he becomes ungrateful for Our blessing as if he sees that blessing as his established right upon Us and sees Us as not owning it. Thus, man is naturally disposed toward despair over what is taken from him and toward ingratitude. The word "man" (الإنسان)—a word indicating his species—is used in the verse to show that what is mentioned of his attribute is from the nature of his species.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَلَئِنْ أَذَقْناهُ نَعْماءَ بَعْدَ ضَرَّاءَ مَسَّتْهُ لَيَقُولَنَّ ذَهَبَ السَّيِّئاتُ عَنِّي إِنَّهُ لَفَرِحٌ فَخُورٌ» (And if We let him taste a blessing after a hardship that has visited him, he will surely say, ‘The evils have left me.’ Indeed, he is exultant and boastful). It is said in *al-Majma‘*: "**Blessing** (النعماء) is a favor whose effect appears on its possessor, and **hardship** (الضراء) is a harm whose state appears through it, because they are expressed in the form of apparent states like *hamra* (red) and *ayna* (large-eyed), along with the exaggeration they contain. **Exultation** (الفرح) and joy are synonyms; it is the opening of the heart by what it finds pleasurable, and its opposite is grief." Until he said: "The **boastful** (الفخور) is one who boasts much, which is to act loftily by enumerating virtues. It is a blameworthy attribute when used absolutely because of the arrogance it contains toward those over whom one should not be arrogant." End quote.

The meaning of "evils" (السيئات), by the context of the situation, refers to the calamities and trials whose descent grieves man. The meaning is: if We strike him with blessing after hardship, he will surely say: "The hardships have left me," which is an allusion (كناية) to the belief that those hardships and calamities will not return after their disappearance nor descend again after their removal.

And His saying: «إِنَّهُ لَفَرِحٌ فَخُورٌ» (Indeed, he is exultant and boastful) is in the position of a causal explanation for His saying: «ذَهَبَ السَّيِّئاتُ عَنِّي» (The evils have left me). For he exults and remains so because of the blessing he tasted after the hardship. If he saw that the blessing he has is liable to vanish, with no certainty of its remaining nor reliance on its permanence, and that the matter is not up to him but to another, and that it is possible for the "evils" he left to return to him, he would not be exultant about it; for there is no exultation in a borrowed matter that has no stability.

And he boasts of the blessing he was given over others. There is no boasting except for an honor or a virtue that a person possesses. Thus, he sees the blessing he has as a matter whose reins are in his hand, which no one else can strip or withdraw from him, nor return to him the "evils" that have left him. Therefore, he boasts and boasts much.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «إِلَّا الَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ أُولئِكَ لَهُمْ مَغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ كَبِيرٌ» (excepting those who are patient and do righteous deeds; for such there will be forgiveness and a great reward). He, the Glorified, mentioned what man is naturally disposed toward during hardship and trial—despair and ingratitude—and during ease and blessing—exultation and boasting. The core of the discourse is that he is a creature of dim sight and short vision, seeing only what he finds in his present state and oblivious to what is beyond that. If a blessing leaves him, he does not see a return for it, nor that it was from Allah, the Glorified; and that He, the Exalted, may return it to him if He wills, so that he might be patient during His trial and his heart might be attached to Him in hope and petition. And if a blessing returns to him after its disappearance, he sees that he owns it, so he exults and boasts, and does not see Allah's handiwork in that so that he might thank Him for it and refrain from exultation and from acting loftily over others through boasting.

He, the Glorified, excepted a group of humans and described them by His saying: «الَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ» (those who are patient and do righteous deeds). Then He promised them a good promise by His saying: «أُولئِكَ لَهُمْ مَغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ كَبِيرٌ» (for such there will be forgiveness and a great reward). This is because escaping this blameworthy nature is only achieved by the patient ones—those who are patient during hardship, so that anxiety does not lead them to despair and ingratitude; and who do righteous deeds of gratitude by praising Him, the Exalted, for removing the hardship and following it with blessing, and by spending His blessings in what pleases Him and benefits His creation, so that self-sufficiency does not lead them to exultation and boasting.

These are the ones who have escaped and are saved; their Lord forgives them by erasing the traces of that blameworthy nature and placing praiseworthy traits in its stead. For them, with their Lord, is forgiveness and a great reward.

The verse contains an indication that patience along with righteous deeds is inseparable from faith (الإيمان), for it promises these patient ones forgiveness and a great reward. Forgiveness is not attained by the polytheists; He, the Exalted, said: «إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ» (Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner should be ascribed to Him) [An-Nisa: 116].

The promise of exactly what is mentioned in this verse—namely forgiveness and a great reward—has appeared for the believers in His saying, the Exalted: «وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ لَهُمْ مَغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ كَبِيرٌ» (and those who have faith and do righteous deeds, for them there will be forgiveness and a great reward) [Fatir: 7], and His saying, the Exalted: «إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُمْ بِالْغَيْبِ لَهُمْ مَغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ كَبِيرٌ» (Indeed those who fear their Lord in secret, for them there will be forgiveness and a great reward) [Al-Mulk: 12].

The connection of these three verses to what preceded them is clear. The discourse in the previous verses was directed at the disbelief of the disbelievers, their labeling the promise of the Resurrection as magic, and their meeting the threat of the descent of punishment with mockery. So He, the Glorified, mentioned that they, in their natural state, do not see the disappearance of the blessing of Allah they possess through the descent of punishment, nor the change of their poor condition into the pleasant life and good enjoyment that Allah promised them at the beginning of the Surah.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «فَلَعَلَّكَ تارِكٌ بَعْضَ ما يُوحى إِلَيْكَ وَضائِقٌ بِهِ صَدْرُكَ» (So maybe you are [inclined to] leave out some of what is revealed to you, and your breast is straitened by it) until the end of the verse. Since the message of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), supported as it was by the Noble Quran, clear signs, proofs, and evidences, was something that no rational person could deny nor any person with sound senses could reject and disbelieve in, what was narrated of the disbelief of the disbelievers and the denial of the polytheists was something far-fetched (مستبعدا) according to nature. When the occurrence of a matter in a certain way is far-fetched, a person begins to explain that matter through a path other than that of far-fetchedness, seeking a way out from attributing the occurrence to what nature finds unlikely.

Since the station in the noble verse is this station, and what Allah, the Glorified, narrated of the disbelief of the deniers and the denial of the polytheists regarding the explicit truth the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) brought to them and the speech of Allah revealed to him—along with the clear signs and proofs that follow it—was something that should not be submitted to because of its natural far-fetchedness, He, the Exalted, explained for that one reason after another by way of expectation (الترجي), saying: «فَلَعَلَّكَ تارِكٌ بَعْضَ ما يُوحى إِلَيْكَ» (So maybe you are leaving out some of what is revealed to you) etc., and «أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَراهُ» (Or do they say, ‘He has fabricated it?’) etc.

It is as if it were said: "It is far-fetched that you guide them to the clear truth and they hear My speech from you, then they do not respond to your call and they disbelieve in the truth after its clarity. So perhaps you are leaving out some of what is revealed to you and not calling them to it, and for that reason, they confronted you with denial. Or do they say that the Quran is not from the speech of Allah, but rather a fabrication you fabricated against Allah, and for that reason, they did not believe in it? If you left out some of the revelation out of fear of their proposing signs to you, then you are only a warner, and you have nothing but what Allah wills. And if they say he fabricated it, then tell them to bring ten surahs like it, fabricated..." etc.

From what has passed, it becomes clear that presenting the discourse in the form of expectation and possibility is to observe what the station requires of the nature of far-fetchedness. The station is one of far-fetchedness, and its requirement is to mention every cause potentially influential in the far-fetched event. Consider this in the case of a king who is informed of the rebellion of some of his weak subjects; he sends one of his agents to call them to hearing and obedience, and writes a letter regarding that, ordering him to read it to them and blaming them for their rebellion and arrogance despite their weakness and humiliation, and despite their master's power, might, and glory. Then the king is informed that they rejected what reached them from him through his messenger. He writes to him a second letter, ordering him to read it to them, and in it: "Perhaps you did not read my letter to them for fear that they would propose to you what you are unable to do, or they claimed that the letter is not from me and that you only fabricated it against me. If it is the first, then you are a messenger whose only duty is the conveyance; and if it is the second, then the letter is in my handwriting, written by my hand and sealed with my seal, and no one other than me can imitate me in that."

Contemplation of this example shows that the station in what the second letter contains of address is a station of far-fetchedness, and that the intent of mentioning the two possibilities—leaving the conveyance and the claim of fabrication—is not to truly rebuke the messenger or to truly consider the claim of lying and fabrication. Rather, the two aspects were mentioned for the purpose of being like a prelude to mentioning what removes the two doubts: namely, that the messenger has no part in the matter such that he could be proposed to with what they propose, and that the letter of the king is beyond doubt or suspicion.

From here, it becomes clear that His saying, the Exalted: «فَلَعَلَّكَ تارِكٌ بَعْضَ ما يُوحى إِلَيْكَ وَضائِقٌ بِهِ صَدْرُكَ» (So maybe you are leaving out some of what is revealed to you, and your breast is straitened by it) etc., does not imply a serious expectation, nor is it directed at rebuking the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), nor is it intended for his consolation and the soothing of his soul following the grief and sorrow that used to reach him due to their disbelief and denial of the explicit truth he brought. Rather, the discourse is directed to lead to the mention of His saying: «إِنَّما أَنْتَ نَذِيرٌ وَاللهُ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ وَكِيلٌ» (You are only a warner, and Allah is guardian over all things).

Thus, what some commentators mentioned—that the discourse is arranged to forbid the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) from grief and straitening of the breast due to the disbelief and denial they confronted him with, and that the prohibition is one of consolation and soothing of the soul, similar to what is in His saying: «وَلا تَحْزَنْ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلا تَكُ فِي ضَيْقٍ مِمَّا يَمْكُرُونَ» (Do not grieve for them, and do not be upset by their plotting) [An-Nahl: 127], and His saying: «لَعَلَّكَ باخِعٌ نَفْسَكَ أَلَّا يَكُونُوا مُؤْمِنِينَ إِنْ نَشَأْ نُنَزِّلْ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنَ السَّماءِ آيَةً فَظَلَّتْ أَعْناقُهُمْ لَها خاضِعِينَ» (You might kill yourself [with grief] that they will not have faith. If We wish, We will send down to them a sign from the sky before which their heads will remain bowed in humility) [Ash-Shu'ara: 3-4]—is a discourse that is out of place.

It also appears that His saying: «فَلَعَلَّكَ تارِكٌ» (So maybe you are leaving out) etc., and His saying: «أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَراهُ» (Or do they say, ‘He has fabricated it?’) etc., are like two sides of a dilemma and both connect to what preceded them from a single perspective, as we mentioned.

And His saying: «تارِكٌ بَعْضَ ما يُوحى إِلَيْكَ» (leaving out some of what is revealed to you)—"some" was mentioned because the previous verses included the conveyance of the revelation in general. That is: perhaps you left out some of what We revealed to you of the Quran and did not recite it to them, so the truth was not fully disclosed to them such that they would not confront you with what they confronted you with of rejection and denial. This is because the Quran, some of it explains others, and one part of it brings another part closer to acceptance, such as the verses of argumentation (الاحتجاج) explaining the verses containing claims, and the verses of reward and punishment bringing the truth closer to acceptance through enticement and intimidation, and the verses of stories and lessons (القصص والعبر) winning over souls and softening hearts.

And His saying: «وَضائِقٌ بِهِ صَدْرُكَ أَنْ يَقُولُوا» (and your breast is straitened by it, that they should say) etc. It is said in *al-Majma‘*: "*Dā’iq* and *dayyiq* (straitened) have the same meaning, except that *dā’iq* here is better for two reasons: one, that it is transitory (عارض), and the other, that it matches the form of *tārik* (leaving out)." End quote.

The apparent meaning is that the pronoun in «بِهِ» (by it) refers to His saying: «بَعْضَ ما يُوحى» (some of what is revealed), although some mentioned that the pronoun refers to their saying: «لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْهِ كَنْزٌ» (Why has a treasure not been sent down to him) etc., or to their proposal. This latter view is more consistent with His saying «أَنْ يَقُولُوا» (that they should say) etc., being a substitute (بدلا) for the pronoun in «بِهِ». What we mentioned is more consistent with it being an object of purpose (مفعولا له) for His saying: «تارِكٌ» (leaving out), and the estimation is: "Perhaps you are leaving that out for fear that they should say: 'Why has a treasure not been sent down to him, or an angel come with him?'"

And His saying: «إِنَّما أَنْتَ نَذِيرٌ» (You are only a warner) is a response to their proposal in their saying: «لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْهِ كَنْزٌ أَوْ جاءَ مَعَهُ مَلَكٌ» (Why has a treasure not been sent down to him, or an angel come with him?). The mention of what they proposed has been repeated in various places of His speech, the Exalted; in some, it was limited to the mention of the coming of an angel, and in others, more was added, such as the proposal to bring Allah, the Glorified, to testify to the message, and that he should have a garden from which he eats, and that he should bring down from the heaven a book they could read. Allah, the Glorified, responded to all of them with the like of what He responded with here: namely, that His messenger has nothing but the message; it is not in his hand, being a human messenger, to respond to what they propose to him unless Allah wills something in that and permits the bringing of a sign, as He said: «وَما كانَ لِرَسُولٍ أَنْ يَأْتِيَ بِآيَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللهِ» (It is not for any apostle to bring a sign except by Allah’s permission) [Al-Mu'min: 78].

Then He followed His saying: «إِنَّما أَنْتَ نَذِيرٌ» (You are only a warner) with His saying: «وَاللهُ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ وَكِيلٌ» (and Allah is guardian over all things) to complete the response to their proposal of miracles to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). Its summary is: the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is a human like them and was only commanded with warning, which is the message by informing of the danger. The undertaking of all matters and their governance (تدبير), whether they run according to custom or are miraculous (خارقة لها), belongs only to Allah, the Glorified; so there is no reason for them to turn to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding what is not his.

This is because Allah, the Glorified, is the Originator of all things and their Creator, and He is the Sustainer of everything in the system that runs upon it. There is nothing except that He, the Exalted, is the beginning in its affair and state, and the end, whether the matters run according to custom or are miraculous. He, the Exalted, is the one to whom its affair is surrendered and who governs its state; thus, He, the Exalted, is the **Guardian** (الوكيل) over it. For the guardian is the one to whom the matter is surrendered and whose judgment is executed within it. Thus, He, the Exalted, is a Guardian over all things.

By this, it becomes clear that His saying: «وَاللهُ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ وَكِيلٌ» (and Allah is guardian over all things), with the aid of His saying: «إِنَّما أَنْتَ نَذِيرٌ» (You are only a warner), implies a restriction of the heart (قصر القلب); for they asked the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) for a matter that was not his, but rather belonged only to Allah.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَراهُ قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِعَشْرِ سُوَرٍ» (Or do they say, ‘He has fabricated it?’ Say, ‘Then bring ten surahs’). Discourse has already passed by which it is correct to take "am" (أَمْ) as connected (متصلة), because His saying: «فَلَعَلَّكَ تارِكٌ» (So maybe you are leaving out) etc., is in the sense of an interrogation. The estimation is: "Are you leaving out some of what is revealed to you for fear of their proposing a miracle, or do they say that you fabricated it against Us?" For it is far-fetched that My speech be read to them then they do not believe in it. It has also been said: "am" is disjunctive (مقطعة), and the meaning is: "Rather, they say he fabricated it."

And His saying: «قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِعَشْرِ سُوَرٍ مِثْلِهِ مُفْتَرَياتٍ» (Say, ‘Then bring ten surahs like it, fabricated’)—in the speech is an explicit challenge (تحد). The pronoun refers to the Quran, or to the Surah insofar as it is Quran. The "fa" in «فَأْتُوا» (Then bring) indicates the branching of the command upon His saying: «افْتَراهُ» (He has fabricated it). In the speech, there is an omission and a connection for the sake of brevity. The estimation is: "Tell them: If this Quran is among what I fabricated against Allah, it was from me, and it would be possible for someone other than me to bring its like. If you are truthful in your claim and serious, not joking, then bring ten surahs like it, fabricated, and seek help in that by calling everyone you can besides Allah—from your idols whom you claim are gods you rush to in needs, and others from the rest of creation—until all causes and means are complete for you, and no one remains whose help might be expected to be effective and whose benefit is hoped for in that. If it were from me and not from Allah, it would then be possible for you to bring its like."

It has become clear by this explanation that the challenge with the Quran in the noble verse is not only from the perspective of its composition and eloquence. For He, the Exalted, orders them to seek help from everyone they can call besides Allah, whether their gods or others, and among them are those who do not know the Arabic language or the excellence of its composition and the quality of its eloquence. Thus, the challenge is general to all that the Noble Quran contains of true knowledge, shining proofs and evidences, good exhortations, noble ethics, divine laws, news of the Unseen, and eloquence and rhetoric, similar to what is in His saying, the Exalted: «قُلْ لَئِنِ اجْتَمَعَتِ الْإِنْسُ وَالْجِنُّ عَلى أَنْ يَأْتُوا بِمِثْلِ هذَا الْقُرْآنِ لا يَأْتُونَ بِمِثْلِهِ وَلَوْ كانَ بَعْضُهُمْ لِبَعْضٍ ظَهِيراً» (Say, ‘Should all humans and jinn rally to bring the like of this Quran, they will not bring its like, even if they assisted one another’) [Al-Isra: 88]. An indication of this has already passed in the discussion on the inimitability (إعجاز) of the Quran in the first volume of the book.

By this, the falsity of the claim that the aspect of the Quran's inimitability is only eloquence and rhetoric in this specific composition becomes clear. For if the aspect of inimitability were other than that, He would not have been satisfied in the counter-challenge with fabrication and invention, because eloquence has three levels: the highest level is inimitable, and the lowest and middle are possible. Thus, the challenge in the verse only occurred in the highest level of it. If the aspect of inimitability were "turning away" (الصرفة) — *the theory that Allah prevented the Arabs from producing the like of the Quran despite their ability* — then feeble speech would be more eloquent in the chapter of inimitability.

The "like" (المثل) mentioned in the verse cannot mean its like in genus (الجنس), because its like in genus would be its narration (حكايته), and the challenge would not occur by it. Rather, it refers to what is customary among the Arabs in challenging one another, as is famous from the poetic contests (مناقضات) of Imru' al-Qays, 'Alqamah, 'Amr ibn Kulthum, al-Harith ibn Hillizah, Jarir, al-Farazdaq, and others. End quote.

For in this claim, firstly: if the aspect of inimitability in the Quran were its eloquence alone—which is a matter unknown to non-Arabs—then including others in the challenge would have no meaning. His saying: «وَادْعُوا مَنِ اسْتَطَعْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ» (and call everyone you can besides Allah), with the generality it contains, and likewise His saying: «لَئِنِ اجْتَمَعَتِ الْإِنْسُ وَالْجِنُ» (Should all humans and jinn rally...) etc., would not lead to a coherent meaning. Rather, it would have been necessary to say: "Should all the Arabs rally," and "call everyone you can from your gods and from the people of your language."

Secondly: if the aspect of inimitability were eloquence only, the argumentation with the like of His saying: «وَلَوْ كانَ مِنْ عِنْدِ غَيْرِ اللهِ لَوَجَدُوا فِيهِ اخْتِلافاً كَثِيراً» (Had it been from [someone] other than Allah, they would surely have found much discrepancy in it) [An-Nisa: 82] would not be correct. This verse is apparent in negating absolute discrepancy, whereas most discrepancies—those referring to meanings—do not harm the eloquence of the wording.

Thirdly: He, the Exalted, challenges with the like of His saying: «فَلْيَأْتُوا بِحَدِيثٍ مِثْلِهِ» (let them bring a discourse like it) [At-Tur: 34], and with His saying in Surah Yunus: «فَأْتُوا بِسُورَةٍ مِثْلِهِ وَادْعُوا مَنِ اسْتَطَعْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ» (then bring a surah like it, and call everyone you can besides Allah) [Yunus: 38]. We have already learned that Surah Yunus was before Surah Hud in the order of revelation, and this is supported by tradition. Then He challenged in this Surah with: «فَأْتُوا بِعَشْرِ سُوَرٍ مِثْلِهِ مُفْتَرَياتٍ وَادْعُوا مَنِ اسْتَطَعْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ» (then bring ten surahs like it, fabricated, and call everyone you can besides Allah). If the aspect of inimitability were eloquence specifically, these challenges would be outside the natural order. For it would not be correct to charge the eloquent ones among the Arabs who deny the Quran being from Allah with bringing one surah like it, then after that with bringing ten fabricated surahs. Rather, nature requires that the challenge be by charging them with bringing the like of the entire Quran; then if they fail, with bringing ten surahs like it, fabricated; then if they fail, with bringing one surah like it.

Some have mentioned, in escaping this problem, that the order between the surahs and the revelation of some before others does not necessitate the order between the verses of the surahs. For many a Meccan verse is placed in a Medinan surah and vice versa. It is possible then that the verses of challenge with the entire Quran were revealed before others absolutely, then the verse of challenge with ten fabricated surahs was revealed after it, and the verse of challenge with one surah was revealed after all of them.

However, this only helps if the revelation of the verses occurred as he imagined; otherwise, the problem remains. The truth is that the Quran is inimitable in all its specific attributes: in its eloquence and rhetoric, in the true knowledge it contains, in its noble ethics, divine laws, stories and lessons, news of the Unseen, and in its authority over hearts and the beauty that rules over souls.

As for the reason for the challenge with ten surahs despite the challenge with one in Surah Yunus, it was said in *al-Majma‘*: "If it is asked: Why was the challenge mentioned once with ten surahs, once with one surah, and once with a discourse like it? The answer is: The challenge only occurs with what manifests inimitability in composed speech; so it is permissible to challenge once with the less and once with the more." End quote.

**I say:** This serves as a reason for the principle of challenging with one or many. But as for the challenge with ten after one—especially according to his view that its inimitability is by eloquence alone—it does not.

Some commentators have suggested, in justifying this, that the Quran is a miracle in everything it contains—including its knowledge (المعارف), ethics (الأخلاق), laws (الأحكام), stories (القصص), and other matters—and it is characterized by eloquence (الفصاحة) and rhetoric (البلاغة) and the absence of contradiction (انتفاء الاختلاف). The validity of a challenge (المعارضة) and the production of something like it (الإتيان بالمثل) only becomes apparent when a number of surahs are produced through which the absence of contradiction is demonstrated—especially among the stories contained therein—alongside other aspects such as eloquence, rhetoric, knowledge, and so forth.

This can only be achieved by producing the likes of the long surahs that encompass all the aforementioned affairs and include knowledge, stories, arguments, and other elements, such as the surahs of *al-A‘raf* and *al-An‘am*.

The long Quranic surahs that were revealed before Surah *Hud*—according to what has been mentioned in the narrations—and which encompass all the aforementioned arts, are: *al-A‘raf*, *Yunus*, *Maryam*, *Ta-Ha*, *al-Shu‘ara’*, *al-Naml*, *al-Qasas*, *al-Qamar*, and *Sad*. These are nine surahs, the tenth being Surah *Hud*. This perspective explains the challenge by their being commanded to bring ten surahs like it, forged. (End of the summary of his words, though he spoke at great length).

I say: Firstly, there is no reliance to be placed on the report (الأثر) he relied upon regarding the order of the revelation of the surahs; it is among the solitary reports (الآحاد) that are not free from weakness, and it is not appropriate to build exegetical research (البحث التفسيري) upon the likes of them.

Secondly, the apparent meaning of His saying: **أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَراهُ قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِعَشْرِ سُوَرٍ مِثْلِهِ مُفْتَرَياتٍ** "Do they say, ‘He has fabricated it?’ Say, ‘Then bring ten surahs like it, fabricated’" (11:13) is that their accusing the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) of fabrication (الافتراء) against Allah, the Glorified, was a claim they made regarding all the Quranic surahs—both the long and the short—without specifying one surah over another. Therefore, it was necessary that they be answered in a way that uproots the doubt regarding every Quranic surah, and the challenge (التحدي) must be sufficient for that. Their inability to bring ten long, forged surahs that combine the Quranic arts does not prove that everything—even the short surahs like *al-Kawthar* and *al-‘Asr*—is from Allah, unless another explanation is joined to it, and the wording is devoid of that.

Thirdly, regarding His saying: **بِعَشْرِ سُوَرٍ مِثْلِهِ** "ten surahs like it," if the pronoun therein refers to the Quran—as is the apparent meaning of this speaker's words—it would imply a challenge to bring ten forged surahs like it absolutely, whether long or short. Thus, restricting the challenge to ten long, comprehensive surahs is a restriction (تقييد) of the verse's wording without a restrictor (مقيد), which is arbitrary (تحكم). Even more arbitrary is the claim that "like it" refers to the likeness of the ten surahs he enumerated.

If the pronoun refers to Surah *Hud*, it would be a preposterous claim. How could it be correct to say to someone who claims that Surah *al-Kawthar* and the *Mu‘awwidhatayn* are fabrications against Allah: "Bring ten forged surahs like Surah *Hud*," and stop at that? Unless they were to babble that Surah *Hud* alone was a fabrication against Allah, the Exalted, in which case they would be challenged to bring its like; however, we have not heard of anyone among them uttering such a thing.

It can be said regarding the variation that appears in the verses of challenge—such as His saying: **فَأْتُوا بِسُورَةٍ مِثْلِهِ** "then bring a surah like it" (10:38), which is apparent in challenging with a single surah; and His saying: **فَأْتُوا بِعَشْرِ سُوَرٍ مِثْلِهِ مُفْتَرَياتٍ** "then bring ten surahs like it, fabricated" (11:13), which is apparent in challenging with a specific number above one; and His saying: **فَلْيَأْتُوا بِحَدِيثٍ مِثْلِهِ** "let them bring a discourse like it" (52:34), which is apparent in challenging with a discourse (حديث) that resembles the Quran even if it is less than a surah—that each of these verses aims at a specific purpose in the challenge.

To explain this: the aspects and affairs of the Quran that constitute its reality as a Divine Book—in addition to the eloquence (الفصاحة) of its wording and the rhetoric (البلاغة) of its composition—ultimately return to its meanings (معانيه) and objectives (مقاصده). I do not mean by "meaning" (المعنى) what the scholars of rhetoric intend when they say: "Rhetoric is an attribute of the meaning, while the words are merely cast on the path," by which they mean concepts (المفاهيم) in terms of their natural order in the mind. For what they mean by "meaning" exists in speech that is an outright lie, in jest, in obscenity, in lampooning, and in slander, if it follows the style of rhetoric; and much of this is found in the speech inherited from the eloquent ones in both poetry and prose.

Rather, what is meant by the meaning and objective of the Quran is what He, the Exalted, describes as being a Wise Book (كتاب حكيم), a Manifest Light (نور مبين), a Grand Quran (قرآن عظيم), a Criterion (الفرقان) — *the decisive standard that distinguishes truth from falsehood* —, a Guide (هاد) that leads to the truth and to a straight path (صراط مستقيم), a Decisive Word (قول فصل) and not a jest, a Mighty Book (كتاب عزيز) that falsehood cannot approach from before it nor from behind it, a Reminder (ذكر), that it judges between people in that which they differ, that it is a healing (شفاء) and a mercy (رحمة) for the believers and increases the wrongdoers (الظالمين) in naught but loss, that it is an exposition (تبيان) of all things, and that none touch it except the purified (المطهرون).

It is evident that all of these are attributes of the meaning (معنى) of the Quran. They are not attributes of what the scholars of rhetoric intend by "eloquent meaning," which might be contained in false speech—which the Quran calls vain talk (لغو), and sin (إثم), and forbids man from engaging in or uttering, even if it be eloquent. Rather, the meaning characterized by these attributes is part of the Divine objectives (المقاصد الإلهية) that proceed upon the truth (الحق) which is unmixed with falsehood, and lie upon the path of guidance (الهداية). Speech containing a meaning of this description and a purpose of this stature is that which the Divine care (العناية الإلهية) is concerned with revealing and making a mercy for the believers and a reminder for all the worlds (العالمين).

This is what is correct to challenge with, as in His saying: **فَلْيَأْتُوا بِحَدِيثٍ مِثْلِهِ** "let them bring a discourse like it." For we do not call speech a "discourse" (حديث) unless it contains an important purpose that is discussed and transmitted from one mind to another. Likewise, His saying: **فَأْتُوا بِسُورَةٍ مِثْلِهِ** "then bring a surah like it," for Allah does not call a group of verses of His Book a "surah" (سورة)—even if they are numerous—unless they contain a Divine purpose (غرض إلهي) by which they are distinguished from others.

Were it not for this, the challenge with the Quranic verses would not be complete, and the opponent would have been able to choose a large number of individual verses, such as His saying: **وَالضُّحى** "By the morning brightness" (93:1), **وَالْعَصْرِ** "By time" (103:1), **وَالطُّورِ** "By the Mount" (52:1), **فِي كِتابٍ مَكْنُونٍ** "in a guarded Book" (56:78), **مُدْهامَّتانِ** "both of them dark green" (55:64), **الْحَاقَّةُ مَا الْحَاقَّةُ وَما أَدْراكَ مَا الْحَاقَّةُ** "The Besieger! What is the Besieger? And what will show you what the Besieger is?" (69:1-3), **الرَّحْمنُ** "The All-beneficent" (55:1), **مَلِكِ النَّاسِ إِلهِ النَّاسِ** "the King of mankind, the God of mankind" (114:2-3), **وَخَسَفَ الْقَمَرُ** "and the moon is eclipsed" (75:8), **كَلَّا وَالْقَمَرِ** "No indeed! By the moon" (74:32), **سَنَدْعُ الزَّبانِيَةَ** "We shall call the keepers of hell" (96:18), and other individual verses, and then match each of them with corresponding Arabic speech, without ensuring a connection between them or their containment of a purpose that unites them and presents them in a form of unity (الوحدة).

Thus, what the opponent was tasked with in these challenges was to bring speech that resembles the Quran, in addition to the rhetoric of its wording, in explaining some of the Divine objectives (المقاصد الإلهية) containing purposes characterized by the attributes mentioned by Allah, the Glorified.

The Divine Speech (الكلام الإلهي), along with what it challenged with in the verses of challenge, varies according to what appears of its specific properties (خاصته). The entirety of the Quran is distinguished by being a Book containing what the human species needs until the Day of Resurrection—in terms of fundamental knowledge (معارف أصلية), noble ethics (أخلاق كريمة), and subsidiary laws (أحكام فرعية). A surah of the Quran is distinguished by a comprehensive explanation of one of the Divine purposes related to guidance (الهدى) and the religion of truth (دين الحق), alongside its miraculous rhetoric (بلاغتها الخارقة); and this is a property different from the property that characterizes the Quran as a whole. A number of surahs, such as ten or twenty, are distinguished by another property: the exposition of various types of objectives and purposes and the diversity (التنوع) within them. This is further removed from the possibility of coincidence (الاتفاق). For if the opponent is unable to bring a single surah, it might occur to his mind that his inability only indicates the inability of people to bring its like, not that it is revealed from Allah, inspired by His knowledge. It might be thought to be like other human attributes and actions, in each of which it is possible for an individual to be unique among the members of the species by chance (اتفاقاً) due to the coincidence of necessitating causes—like a human individual described as being the tallest, or having the largest body, or being the bravest, most generous, most cowardly, or most miserly.

This possibility—even if it is refuted regarding a single surah of the Quran that the opponent intends to challenge—for it is eloquent speech containing true meanings with noble attributes, free from the material of falsehood, and such a thing does not occur by mere chance and accident (الصدفة) without being intended in itself with a purpose related to the will (الإرادة)—

However, the aforementioned possibility of chance and accident is even further removed regarding multiple surahs. For bringing surah after surah, explaining purpose after purpose, and uncovering one hidden truth after another, leaves no room for the possibility of chance and accident; this is obvious.

When what we have mentioned becomes clear, it becomes evident that it is possible for the challenge in His saying: **قُلْ لَئِنِ اجْتَمَعَتِ الْإِنْسُ وَالْجِنُّ عَلى أَنْ يَأْتُوا بِمِثْلِ هذَا الْقُرْآنِ لا يَأْتُونَ بِمِثْلِهِ وَلَوْ كانَ بَعْضُهُمْ لِبَعْضٍ ظَهِيراً** "Say, ‘Should all humans and jinn rally to bring the like of this Quran, they will not bring its like, even if they assisted one another’" (17:88) to be a challenge regarding the entire Quran for all the Divine purposes it has gathered, and it is distinguished by being a compendium of everything people need until the Day of Resurrection. And His saying: **قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِسُورَةٍ مِثْلِهِ** "Say, ‘Then bring a surah like it’" (10:38) is due to the manifest property within it, which is that it contains a complete and comprehensive explanation of one of the purposes of Divine guidance—a decisive explanation without jest. And His saying: **قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِعَشْرِ سُوَرٍ** "Say, ‘Then bring ten surahs’" (11:13) is a challenge with ten Quranic surahs because of the variety in exposition and diversity in purposes due to the quantity. "Ten" (العشرة) is among the words used for multiplicity (الكثرة), like a hundred or a thousand.

The Exalted said: **يَوَدُّ أَحَدُهُمْ لَوْ يُعَمَّرُ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ** "Each of them is eager to live a thousand years" (2:96).

So, what is meant by "ten surahs"—and Allah knows best—is many surahs that possess some of the levels of multiplicity known among people. It is as if it were said: "Bring a number of its surahs, let them be ten, so that it may become clear that the diversity of Quranic purposes in its miraculous exposition is only from Allah."

As for His saying: **فَلْيَأْتُوا بِحَدِيثٍ مِثْلِهِ** "let them bring a discourse like it" (52:34), it is as if it were a challenge encompassing the three previous challenges. For "discourse" (الحديث) includes a surah, ten surahs, and the entire Quran; thus, it is a challenge with the absolute Quranic property (الخاصة القرآنية), and this is apparent.

Two matters remain here. First: in none of the mentioned verses of challenge was what the opponent brings described as "fabricated" (الافتراء) except in this verse, where it is said: **فَأْتُوا بِعَشْرِ سُوَرٍ مِثْلِهِ مُفْتَرَياتٍ** "Then bring ten surahs like it, fabricated," unlike His saying: **فَأْتُوا بِسُورَةٍ مِثْلِهِ** "Then bring a surah like it," where it did not say: "bring a surah like it, fabricated," and similarly in the other verses of challenge.

Perhaps the reason for this is that the type of concern (العناية) in the verse under discussion is different from the type of concern in the other verses of challenge. The concern in the other verses is related to the fact that they are unable to bring the like of the Quran or the like of a surah because it is a Quran containing aspects that human power cannot reach, and none but Allah, the Exalted, can produce. The statement there was made absolutely.

As for this verse, since it was followed by His saying: **فَإِلَّمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا لَكُمْ فَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّما أُنْزِلَ بِعِلْمِ اللهِ** "But if they do not respond to you, then know that it has been sent down by Allah’s knowledge" (11:14), it indicates that the challenge therein is based on the Quran containing that which is exclusive to the knowledge of Allah, to which no one else has access. This is something that, by its very nature, does not accept fabrication (الافتراء). It is as if it were said: "This Quran, by its very nature, does not accept fabrication, for it contains matters of Divine knowledge (العلم الإلهي) to which none but He, the Exalted, has access. If you are in doubt about that, then bring ten surahs like it, fabricated—which you claim to be fabrication—and seek help from whomever you can besides Allah. If you are unable to do so, then know that it is from the knowledge exclusive to Him, the Exalted." Understand this.

Second: the meaning of the challenge with "the like" (المثل) where it is said: **بِمِثْلِ هذَا الْقُرْآنِ** "the like of this Quran," **بِحَدِيثٍ مِثْلِهِ** "a discourse like it," **بِسُورَةٍ مِثْلِهِ** "a surah like it," **بِعَشْرِ سُوَرٍ مِثْلِهِ** "ten surahs like it." The apparent reason for this is that since the speech was a miraculous sign (آية معجزة), if a human were to bring what resembles it (يماثله), it would suffice to invalidate its being a miraculous sign; there would be no need to bring something that surpasses it in its attributes or excels it in its properties.

It might be objected that the inability of anyone other than him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to do that does not prove it is a miracle not attributed to him. For the attributes of perfection (صفات الكمال) found in the human species—such as eloquence, writing, bravery, generosity, and others—have varying and different levels, some excelling others. If this is the case, there must be a level that is above all others, which is the ultimate point to which the human soul can possibly ascend.

Thus, for every attribute of perfection, there exists among the individuals characterized by it one who carries the highest degree and the furthest limit of it, such that no one else equals him or opposes him. Necessarily, among human beings in general, there is one who is the most eloquent, the best writer, the bravest, or the most generous, just as there is among them one who is the tallest in stature or the largest in body. Why is it not possible that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was the most articulate and eloquent of all people, and the Quran is from his speech, which no one can oppose because he stands in a position where no one else has a foothold? In that case, the inability of others to bring its like would not be proof of its being Divine speech and not human, for it could be human speech exclusive to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and withheld from others.

This is refuted by the fact that human attributes in which excellence (التفاضل) occurs—even if they are as mentioned—are nonetheless things that human nature (الطبيعة الإنسانية) allows for through the potential (الاستعداد) Allah has placed within it, without arising from mere chance and without a cause that enables the characterized individual to possess them.

If this is the case, and an individual is assumed to be exclusive in a superior attribute that no one else equals or surpasses, it would be possible for others to follow the path he paved and become accustomed—through practice (التمرن), training (التدرب), and discipline (الارتياض)—to the actions that stem from the attribute of perfection he possesses.
