Thus, they could bring something resembling (يماثل) some of the perfection he is exclusive in and imitate him in a portion of his works, even if they cannot compete with him in everything or resemble him in the whole. The aforementioned genius individual would retain the position of originality (الأصالة), precedence (السبقة), and advancement in that. For example, *Hatim* [al-Ta'i], even if he was unique and unopposed in his generosity and bounty such that no one else could surpass him, it is possible for a disciplined person to follow his path, train, and practice in it, and thus bring something of the type of his generosity and bounty, even if he cannot compete with him in everything or in his original standing. All human perfections that are sources of actions follow this path; man is able, through practice and training, to follow the path of the preceding innovators therein and bring something of their works, even if he cannot compete with them in their original position.

So, if the Quran were from the speech of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—on the assumption that he was the most eloquent and articulate of humans—it would have been possible for someone else to take interest and train himself to follow the wondrous composition (النظم البديع) he innovated in his speech. He would then be able to imitate him in some of the speech and bring some discourse in a surah like it, even if he could not imitate the entire Quran or bring all of it.

He did not say in what He challenged with: "Let them bring a discourse more eloquent than it or better," or "a surah that is more eloquent or better," such that it could be said: "The Quran is the most eloquent or best human speech, and there is nothing more eloquent or better than it for anyone to bring," and thus the inability to bring that would not prove it is speech of other than humans. Rather, He said: **فَلْيَأْتُوا بِحَدِيثٍ مِثْلِهِ** "let them bring a discourse like it," **قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِسُورَةٍ مِثْلِهِ** "Say, ‘Then bring a surah like it’," and so on. It is within human power to bring the like (مثل) of the speech of another human, even if that other is assumed to have a standing in speech that no one else opposes, as we have explained. Thus, the doubt is refuted by His saying, the Exalted: **مِثْلِهِ** "like it."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَإِلَّمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا لَكُمْ فَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّما أُنْزِلَ بِعِلْمِ اللهِ وَأَنْ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ فَهَلْ أَنْتُمْ مُسْلِمُونَ** "But if they do not respond to you, then know that it has been sent down by Allah’s knowledge, and that there is no god except Him. Will you, then, submit?" (11:14). "Answering the call" (إجابة الدعوة) and "responding" (استجابتها) carry the same meaning.

It is apparent from the context that the address in the verse is to the polytheists (المشركين), and that it is a continuation of the speech of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) which he was commanded to deliver to them by His saying: **قُلْ** "Say." Accordingly, the plural pronoun in His saying: **فَإِلَّمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا** "But if they do not respond" refers to the deities and everyone they sought help from, who are indicated by His saying: **وَادْعُوا مَنِ اسْتَطَعْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ** "and invoke whomever you can besides Allah" (11:13).

The meaning is: If they do not respond to you, O assembly of polytheists—these whom you have called upon from among your deities, the eloquent ones of your tongue who are knowledgeable in the styles of speech, the scholars of the People of the Book who possess the Heavenly Books and the reports of the prophets and nations, the soothsayers who draw support from the suggestions of the devils among the jinn, and the masters of knowledge and understanding from among other people who delve into the various aspects of human knowledge—then know that this Quran was only sent down by the knowledge of Allah (بعلم الله). It was not fabricated from my own knowledge, nor from the knowledge of anyone else whom you claim teaches me and dictates to me. And know also that what I call you to of monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — is the truth. For if there were a god besides Allah, he would have helped you in what you called him to. Will you then, O polytheists, be submissive (مسلمون) to Allah, the Exalted, and compliant with His command?

Thus, His saying, the Exalted: **فَإِلَّمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا لَكُمْ** "But if they do not respond to you" is in the sense of our saying: "If you are unable to challenge it after seeking help and support from whomever you could call upon besides Allah." This is because the causes (الأسباب) that would necessitate their ability to challenge are the power of expression and the genius of eloquence they possess—and they believe that these are gifts from their deities besides Allah—as well as what their deities possess which they have not yet bestowed upon them but could support them with if they wished, according to their claim; and also the help from those other than their deities. If those they call upon do not respond to them in challenging the Quran, then all the causes necessitating their ability have been removed, and thereby their ability itself is removed. Thus, the failure of the partners to respond to the challenge of the Quran is inseparable from their own inability to do so, even with the power they themselves possess. So, there is a metonymy (كناية) in the speech.

And His saying: **فَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّما أُنْزِلَ بِعِلْمِ اللهِ** "then know that it has been sent down by Allah’s knowledge." It is apparent that what is meant by "Allah's knowledge" is the knowledge exclusive to Him, which is the Unseen (الغيب) — *that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge* — to which no one else has access except by His permission, as He, the Exalted, said: **لكِنِ اللهُ يَشْهَدُ بِما أَنْزَلَ إِلَيْكَ أَنْزَلَهُ بِعِلْمِهِ** "But Allah bears witness to what He has sent down to you—He sent it down with His knowledge" (4:166); and He said: **ذلِكَ مِنْ أَنْباءِ الْغَيْبِ نُوحِيهِ إِلَيْكَ** "That is from the news of the Unseen which We reveal to you" (12:102); and He said: **عالِمُ الْغَيْبِ فَلا يُظْهِرُ عَلى غَيْبِهِ أَحَداً إِلَّا مَنِ ارْتَضى مِنْ رَسُولٍ** "Knower of the Unseen, He does not disclose His Unseen to anyone, except to an apostle He approves of" (72:26-27); and He said: **إِنَّهُ لَقُرْآنٌ كَرِيمٌ فِي كِتابٍ مَكْنُونٍ لا يَمَسُّهُ إِلَّا الْمُطَهَّرُونَ تَنْزِيلٌ مِنْ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ** "This is indeed a noble Quran, in a guarded Book—no one touches it except the pure—a revelation from the Lord of all the worlds" (56:77-80).

The meaning is: If you are unable to challenge it by any supporting cause you have clung to besides Allah, then be certain that it was not sent down except from an Unseen cause, and that it is from the news of the Unseen which is exclusive to Him, the Exalted. He is the one who sent it down to me, spoke to me with it, and intended for me and for you to understand the true knowledge and treasures of guidance within it.

Some have mentioned that the meaning is that it was sent down based on Allah's knowledge of its revelation and His testimony for it. Others mentioned that the meaning is that it was sent down with the knowledge from Allah that it does not accept challenge, or with the knowledge from Allah of its composition and order, which no one else knows. These are weak meanings, far from the [correct] understanding.

The sentence—meaning His saying: **أَنَّما أُنْزِلَ بِعِلْمِ اللهِ** "that it has been sent down by Allah’s knowledge"—is one of the two conclusions drawn from the failure of their partners to respond to them. The other conclusion is His saying: **وَأَنْ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ** "and that there is no god except Him." The necessity of this conclusion arises from two aspects: First, when they called upon their deities for their important affairs and they did not respond, it revealed that they are not gods. For the [true] God is only He who responds to the distressed when he calls upon Him, especially if he calls Him for something that benefits the deity called upon. For the Quran brought by the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was cutting off their roots, killing their mention, and turning people away from them. If they did not respond to their devotees when they called them to challenge a Book of this nature, it was the clearest evidence for the negation of their divinity.

Second, if it is established that the Quran is the truth revealed from Allah, truthful in what it informs, and among what it informs is that there is no other god with Allah, then it is known thereby that there is no god but Allah, the Glorified.

And His saying: **فَهَلْ أَنْتُمْ مُسْلِمُونَ** "Will you, then, submit?"—meaning: since you have known and it has become clear to you through the failure of your partners besides Allah to respond and your own inability to challenge, will you then be submissive (مسلمون) to what your knowledge has settled upon regarding the monotheism of Allah, the Glorified, and the fact that this Quran is a Book revealed by His knowledge? This is a command to submit (الإسلام) in the form of an interrogation. All of this is what the apparent meaning of the verse requires.

It has been said that the address in His saying: **فَإِلَّمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا لَكُمْ** "But if they do not respond to you" etc., is to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), addressed in the plural form to honor him and magnify his status, and the third-person plural pronoun refers to the polytheists. That is: "If the polytheists do not respond to what you have called them to of challenging it, O Prophet, then know that it is revealed by Allah's knowledge and that Allah is One; so are you submissive to His command?"

The problem with this is that it is established that honorifics using the plural form and multiplicity are exclusive in Arabic speech to the speaker (المتكلم); as for the second person (الخطاب) and third person (الغيبة), there is no honoring in them through the plural form.

In addition, the attribution of Divine revelation (الوحي الإلهي) and Lordly speech (التكليم الرباني) to Him, the Exalted, is a necessary attribution that does not accept doubt such that it would require help from evidence. What the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) receives, its indication of being speech from Allah is a necessary indication (دلالة ضرورية) that does not need an argument (حجة) such that it would be argued for by the polytheists' failure to respond to the challenge of the Quran and their inability to do so. This is unlike the speech of creatures—humans, jinn, angels, or any other caller—for obtaining knowledge of its attribution to its speaker requires external evidence from the senses or the intellect. An indication of this was previously mentioned in the story of *Zakariyya* in Surah *Al ‘Imran*, and a full discussion of this will come in its appropriate place, Allah willing.

Furthermore, addressing the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with the likes of His saying: **وَأَنْ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ** "and that there is no god except Him," and His saying: **فَهَلْ أَنْتُمْ مُسْلِمُونَ** "Will you, then, submit?" is not free from preposterousness. Moreover, the reasoning itself is incomplete, as we shall explain.

It has also been said that the address in the verse is to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the believers together, or to the believers specifically, because the believers share with him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in the religious call and the challenge with the Quran, which is the Book of their Lord revealed to them. The meaning would be: "If the polytheists do not respond to you in the challenge, then know that the Quran is revealed by Allah's knowledge and that there is no god but Him; so will you submit to Allah?"

When some realized that there is no meaning in calling the believers—while they are believers in Allah alone and in His Book—to the knowledge that it is a Book revealed from Allah and that He, the Exalted, is One without partner, they corrected it by saying the meaning is: "Then remain firm in your knowledge that it was only sent down by Allah's knowledge, and increase thereby in faith and certainty, and that there is no god but Him and none deserves worship besides Him; so are you firm in your submission (إسلامكم) and sincerity therein?"

The problem with this is that it is a restriction (تقييد) of the verse without a restrictor, and the argument is incomplete. This is because if the polytheists had taken the position of challenge with the resources they had and sought help therein by calling upon their deities and others they hoped for among jinn and humans, and then failed, that would have been clear evidence indicating to them that the Quran is above human speech, and the argument would have been completed against them. As for the disbelievers' failure to respond to the challenge, it does not prove it is from Allah, because they did not comply with what they were commanded by His saying: **فَأْتُوا بِعَشْرِ سُوَرٍ مِثْلِهِ مُفْتَرَياتٍ** "Then bring ten surahs like it, fabricated." This was either because they knew it was the true speech of Allah and their saying: **افْتَراهُ** "He has fabricated it" was a statement arising from stubbornness (العناد) and persistence (اللجاج), not from conviction or doubt; or because they were despairing of their partners responding to the call for challenge; or because they were jesting in saying that, babbling nonsense.

In short, the polytheists' failure to respond to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), or to the believers, or to all of them, does not in itself prove that the Quran is revealed from Allah, unless that failure to respond occurred after they had actually called their partners to the challenge and they failed to respond to them. No such call was realized from the polytheists. The mere failure of the polytheists themselves to respond is of no use. It remains only to be said that the meaning of the verse is: "If the polytheists call upon whomever they can besides Allah and they do not respond to them, and the polytheists do not respond to you, O Prophet and assembly of believers, then know that it was sent down by Allah's knowledge," etc. This is what we alluded to just now as being a restriction of the verse without a restrictor.

Furthermore, it contains a command to the believers to be guided in their faith and certainty by a hypothetical matter that did not occur, and His speech, the Exalted, is far above that. If the intention was to indicate that they are unable to do that even if they called their partners to the challenge, it would have been proper for the speech to say: "But if they do not respond to you—and they will never respond—then know," etc., as was said in its counterpart: **وَإِنْ كُنْتُمْ فِي رَيْبٍ مِمَّا نَزَّلْنا عَلى عَبْدِنا فَأْتُوا بِسُورَةٍ مِنْ مِثْلِهِ وَادْعُوا شُهَداءَكُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ فَإِنْ لَمْ تَفْعَلُوا وَلَنْ تَفْعَلُوا فَاتَّقُوا النَّارَ الَّتِي وَقُودُهَا النَّاسُ وَالْحِجارَةُ أُعِدَّتْ لِلْكافِرِينَ** "And if you are in doubt concerning what We have sent down to Our servant, then bring a surah like it, and invoke your witnesses besides Allah, should you be truthful. But if you do not—and you will not—then beware the Fire whose fuel is humans and stones, prepared for the faithless" (2:23-24).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **مَنْ كانَ يُرِيدُ الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا وَزِينَتَها نُوَفِّ إِلَيْهِمْ أَعْمالَهُمْ فِيها وَهُمْ فِيها لا يُبْخَسُونَ** "As for those who desire the life of this world and its glitter, We shall pay them in full for their works here, and they shall not be defrauded here" (11:15). "Paying in full" (التوفية) is delivering the right to its owner and giving it to him completely. "Defrauding" (البخس) is the reduction of the reward.

In the verse is a threat to those who do not submit to the truth when it comes to them and do not surrender to it, preferring the life of this world and forgetting the Hereafter. It is an exposition of something from the sunnah of causes (سنة الأسباب) that condemns them to despair of the bliss of the life of the Hereafter.

This is because an action, whatever it may be, only grants man the goal he intended by it and for which he performed it. If it was a worldly goal—to rectify the affairs of the life of this world such as wealth, beauty, or a good state—the action leads him—if other active causes assist—to what he hopes for by the action. As for the goals of the Hereafter, there is no news of them because they were not intended so as to occur. The mere suitability of an action to lie on the path of the Hereafter and benefit in attaining its bliss—such as righteousness (البر), kindness (الإحسان), and good character—does not necessitate reward and the raising of degrees as long as it was not intended for the sake of Allah and the abode of His reward.

Therefore, He followed it with His saying, the Exalted: **أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ لَيْسَ لَهُمْ فِي الْآخِرَةِ إِلَّا النَّارُ وَحَبِطَ ما صَنَعُوا فِيها وَباطِلٌ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ** "They are the ones for whom there shall be nothing in the Hereafter but the Fire: what they have wrought here will come to naught, and what they used to do will be in vain" (11:16). He informed that when they enter the life of the Hereafter, they will fall into an abode whose reality is a fire that consumes all their actions in life just as fire consumes wood, and it destroys and annihilates everything that their souls found pleasant of the beauties of existence. It causes everything they wrought therein to come to naught (حبط) and invalidates what they sent forth of actions in the world. Therefore, He, the Glorified, called it in another place the "Abode of Ruin" (دار البوار)—meaning destruction—saying: **أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ بَدَّلُوا نِعْمَتَ اللهِ كُفْراً وَأَحَلُّوا قَوْمَهُمْ دارَ الْبَوارِ جَهَنَّمَ يَصْلَوْنَها** "Have you not regarded those who substituted ungratefulness for Allah’s blessing, and settled their people in the abode of ruin? Hell, which they shall enter" (14:28-29). Thereby it becomes clear that both His saying: **وَحَبِطَ ما صَنَعُوا فِيها** "what they have wrought here will come to naught" and His saying: **وَباطِلٌ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ** "and what they used to do will be in vain" explain His saying: **أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ لَيْسَ لَهُمْ فِي الْآخِرَةِ إِلَّا النَّارُ** "They are the ones for whom there shall be nothing in the Hereafter but the Fire" with a certain type of explanation.

From what has preceded, it becomes clear, firstly: that what is meant by "paying them in full for their works" is paying them the results of their works and delivering the effects they have according to the system of causes and effects (نظام الأسباب والمسببات), not what the actor intends by his act and hopes for by his endeavor. For what the actor attains in this world by his act is the result of the act which other active causes assist him in, not whatever the actor aims for; for not everything a man wishes for does he attain.

Allah, the Exalted, expressed this reality in another place by His saying: **وَمَنْ كانَ يُرِيدُ حَرْثَ الدُّنْيا نُؤْتِهِ مِنْها وَما لَهُ فِي الْآخِرَةِ مِنْ نَصِيبٍ** "and whoever desires the tillage of the world, We give him of it, and he will have no share in the Hereafter" (42:20). He, the Exalted, said: **نُؤْتِهِ مِنْها** "We give him of it," and did not say: "We give it to him." And He said in another place: **مَنْ كانَ يُرِيدُ الْعاجِلَةَ عَجَّلْنا لَهُ فِيها ما نَشاءُ لِمَنْ نُرِيدُ ثُمَّ جَعَلْنا لَهُ جَهَنَّمَ يَصْلاها مَذْمُوماً مَدْحُوراً** "Whoever desires the transitory [life], We expedite for him therein whatever We wish, for whomever We desire. Then We appoint hell for him, to enter it, blamed and spurned" (18:18). He mentioned what man desires of the world and attains from it, and added an explanation that not everyone who desires a matter attains it, nor is everything desired attained; rather, the matter is up to Allah, the Glorified: He gives what He wills and withholds what He wills, and He advances whom He wills and delays whom He wills, according to how the sunnah of causes proceeds.

Secondly: the two verses—meaning His saying: **مَنْ كانَ يُرِيدُ الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا وَزِينَتَها نُوَفِّ إِلَيْهِمْ أَعْمالَهُمْ** "As for those who desire the life of this world and its glitter, We shall pay them in full for their works" until the end of the two verses—explain one of the Divine realities.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Kafi*: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **أَلا إِنَّهُمْ يَثْنُونَ صُدُورَهُمْ** "Behold! They fold up their breasts" (11:5), with its chain of narration from Ibn Mahbub, from Jamil ibn Salih, from Sudayr, from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him), who said: "Jabir ibn ‘Abdullah informed me that the polytheists, when they passed by the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) around the House, one of them would lower his head and back like this—and cover his head with a garment so that the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) would not see him—so Allah revealed: **أَلا إِنَّهُمْ يَثْنُونَ** (Behold! They fold up)."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Abu al-Shaykh narrated from Abu Razin, who said: "One of them would bend his back and cover himself with his garment."

In *al-Majma‘*: It is narrated from ‘Ali ibn al-Husayn, Abu Ja‘far, and Ja‘far ibn Muhammad (peace be upon them): **يثنوني** on the pattern of *yaf‘aw‘ilu*.

In the *Tafsir* of al-‘Ayyashi: from Muhammad ibn al-Fudayl, from Jabir, from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him), who said: "A man from the desert came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, I have sons and daughters, brothers and sisters—and grandsons and granddaughters, and nephews and nieces—and our livelihood is meager. If you see fit, O Messenger of Allah, to pray to Allah to expand [our provision] for us—'"

He said: "And he wept, so the Muslims felt pity for him. Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: **ما مِنْ دَابَّةٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ ـ إِلَّا عَلَى اللهِ رِزْقُها وَيَعْلَمُ مُسْتَقَرَّها وَمُسْتَوْدَعَها ـ كُلٌّ فِي كِتابٍ مُبِينٍ** 'There is no animal on the earth, but that its sustenance is on Allah, and He knows its habitation and its repository: all is in a manifest Book' (11:6). Whoever takes charge of these mouths—whose sustenance is guaranteed by Allah—Allah will pour sustenance upon him in abundance—like pouring water. If it is little, then a little; and if it is much, then much." He said: "Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) prayed, and the Muslims said 'Amen' for him."

He said: Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) said: "One who saw the man in the time of ‘Umar narrated to me; he asked him about his state, and he said: '[I am] among the best of his people in lawful wealth and the most abundant of them in riches.'"

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: al-Hakim al-Tirmidhi in *Nawadir al-Usul*, al-Hakim (who authenticated it), Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in *Shu‘ab al-Iman* narrated from Ibn Mas‘ud, from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), who said: "When the appointed time (أجل) of one of you is in a land, a need is facilitated for him toward it—until when he reaches the furthest extent of his trace therein, he is taken—then the earth says on the Day of Resurrection: 'This is what you deposited with me (استودعتني).'"

**I say:** The narration is not clearly an explanation of the verse.

In *al-Kafi*: with its chain of narration from Abu Hamzah al-Thumali, from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said during the Farewell Pilgrimage: 'Behold, the Trustworthy Spirit (الروح الأمين) breathed into my heart—that no soul shall die until it has completed its sustenance (رزقها)—so be God-conscious (اتقوا الله) and be moderate in seeking. Let not the slowness of any sustenance lead you—to seek it through an act of disobedience to Allah—for Allah, the Exalted, has divided the sustenances among His creation as lawful (حلالاً)—and He has not divided them as unlawful (حراماً). So whoever is God-conscious and patient, his sustenance will come to him from its lawful source; and whoever tears the veil of Allah's protection, Mighty and Majestic is He—and takes it from other than its lawful source, it will be deducted from his lawful sustenance—and he will be held accountable for it.'"

**I say:** The narration is among the famous ones, narrated by both the general and specific [scholars] through many paths.

In the *Tafsir* of al-‘Ayyashi: from Abu al-Hudhayl, from Abu ‘Abdullah (peace be upon him), who said: "Allah has divided the sustenances among His servants—and He has bestowed a great surplus which He has not divided among anyone. Allah said: **وَسْئَلُوا اللهَ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ** 'and ask Allah of His grace' (4:32)."

**I say:** The narration is narrated from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and some reports of this meaning have preceded under His saying, the Exalted: **وَتَرْزُقُ مَنْ تَشاءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسابٍ** "and You provide for whomever You wish without any reckoning" (3:27), and His saying, the Exalted: **وَسْئَلُوا اللهَ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ** "and ask Allah of His grace" (4:32).

In *al-Kafi*: from Abu ‘Abdullah (peace be upon him), who said: "Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) used to say often: 'Know with certain knowledge that Allah, Majestic and Sublime is He, has not allowed for a servant—even if his effort is intense, his ingenuity great, and his schemes numerous—to precede what has been named for him in the Wise Reminder (الذكر الحكيم). O people, no man shall increase by a speck through his cleverness, nor shall any man decrease by a speck due to his foolishness—so the one who knows this and acts upon it is the most relaxed of people in his benefit—and the one who knows this and abandons it is the most occupied of people in his harm. Many a person who is blessed is being led to ruin (مستدرج) through the kindness shown to him—and many a person who is deceived among people is being favored—.'"

"So be God-conscious, O striver, regarding your striving, and curtail your haste, and wake up from the slumber of your heedlessness—and reflect upon what has come from Allah, Mighty and Majestic is He, upon the tongue of His Prophet (صلى الله عليه واله وسلم)." (The tradition).

In *al-Kafi*: with its chain of narration from Ibn Abi ‘Umayr, from ‘Abdullah ibn al-Hajjaj, from Abu ‘Abdullah (peace be upon him), who said: "Muhammad ibn al-Munkadir used to say: 'I did not think that ‘Ali ibn al-Husayn would leave behind a creation—better than him until I saw his son Muhammad ibn ‘Ali—so I wanted to admonish him, but he admonished me—.' His companions said to him: 'With what did he admonish you?' He said: 'I went out to some outskirts of Medina at a hot hour—and I met Abu Ja‘far Muhammad ibn ‘Ali, who was a stout and heavy man—leaning on two black youths or two freed slaves—so I said to myself: "Glory be to Allah—a sheikh from the sheikhs of Quraysh at this hour in such a state—seeking the world? Indeed, I shall admonish him—."'"

"So I approached him and greeted him, and he returned the greeting with a panting breath while he was dripping with sweat—so I said: 'May Allah rectify you, a sheikh from the sheikhs of Quraysh at this hour—in this state seeking the world—have you considered if your appointed time (أجلك) came while you were in this state?' He said: 'If death came to me while I was in this state—it would come to me while I am in an act of obedience from the acts of obedience to Allah, Mighty and Majestic is He—by which I protect myself and my family from you and from the people. I only feared that death would come to me—while I was in an act of disobedience from the acts of disobedience to Allah.' So I said: 'You have spoken the truth, may Allah have mercy on you—I wanted to admonish you, but you admonished me.'"

In the same [source]: with its chain of narration from ‘Abd al-A‘la, the freed slave of the family of Sam, who said: "I met Abu ‘Abdullah in one of the paths of Medina—on a summer day of intense heat—so I said: 'May I be your ransom, your status with Allah, Mighty and Majestic is He—and your kinship to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—and yet you exert yourself on such a day?' He said: 'O ‘Abd al-A‘la, I went out seeking sustenance (الرزق)—to be independent thereby of the likes of you.'"

**I say:** There is no contradiction between the decree (القضاء) of sustenance and the command to seek it. This is obvious.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: al-Tayalisi, Ahmad, al-Tirmidhi (who graded it *hasan*), Ibn Majah, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Abu al-Shaykh in *al-‘Azamah*, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Asma’ wa al-Sifat* narrated from Abu Razin, who said: "I said: 'O Messenger of Allah, where was our Lord—before He created His creation?' He said: 'He was in a cloud (عماء), below which was air and above which was air, and He created His Throne upon the water.'"

**I say:** **العماء** (the cloud) is the mist that prevents sight from penetrating it. The "what" (ما) in his saying: "what was below it was air and what was above it was air" is a relative pronoun, and what is meant by "air" (الهواء) is that which is empty of everything, as in His saying, the Exalted: **وَأَفْئِدَتُهُمْ هَواءٌ** "and their hearts are empty" (14:43). Or it is a negating particle, and what is meant by "air" is its well-known meaning; the meaning being that it was a cloud that the air did not encompass, unlike all other clouds.

The narration is among the reports of anthropomorphism (التجسم), and therefore it was interpreted that his saying: "in a cloud" etc., is a metonymy for the Unseen of the Essence (غيب الذات) before which sights fail and intellects are bewildered.

In the same [source]: Ahmad, al-Bukhari, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa’i, Abu al-Shaykh in *al-‘Azamah*, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Asma’ wa al-Sifat* narrated from ‘Imran ibn Husayn, who said: "The people of Yemen said: 'O Messenger of Allah, inform us about the beginning of this matter—how was it?' He said: 'Allah was before everything, and His Throne was upon the water, and He wrote in the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ) the mention of everything, and He created the heavens and the earth.' Then a caller called out: 'Your camel has gone, O son of Husayn!'—so I went off and behold, the mirage was cutting off [the view] before it—by Allah, I wished that I had left it."

**I say:** Several traditionists narrated this narration from Buraydah; and Buraydah said at the end of it: "Then a comer came to me and said: 'This camel of yours has gone'—so I went out and the mirage was cutting off [the view] before it—and I wished that I had left it." This is among the things that weaken the two traditions.

In the same [source]: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلاً** "that He may test you [to see] which of you is best in conduct" (11:7): Dawud ibn al-Muhabbir in the *Book of Intellect*, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Hakim in the *History*, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn ‘Umar, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) recited this verse: **لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلاً** so I said: 'What is the meaning of that, O Messenger of Allah?' He said: 'That He may test you which of you is best in intellect (عقلاً).' Then he said: 'And the best of you in intellect is the most wary of you (أورعكم) regarding the prohibitions of Allah—and the most knowledgeable of you (أعلمكم) regarding the obedience of Allah.'"

In *al-Kafi*: narrated from Sufyan ibn ‘Uyaynah, from Abu ‘Abdullah (peace be upon him): regarding the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic is He: **لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلاً** he said: "He said: 'It does not mean the one of you with the most actions—but the one of you with the most correct action (أصوبكم عملاً). Correctness is only the fear of Allah and the sincere intention (النية الصادقة).'"

Then he said: "Maintaining the action—until it is sincere is harder than the action itself. The sincere action (العمل الخالص) is that which you do not want anyone to praise you for—except Allah, Mighty and Majestic is He. And the intention (النية) is better than the action—behold, the intention is the action—." Then he recited the saying of the Mighty and Majestic: **قُلْ كُلٌّ يَعْمَلُ عَلى شاكِلتِهِ** "Say, ‘Everyone acts according to his character’" (17:84)—meaning according to his intention.

**I say:** His saying "behold, the intention is the action" means that the action has no effect except for what it possesses of intention.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Nu‘mani: with its chain of narration from Ishaq ibn ‘Abd al-‘Aziz, from Abu ‘Abdullah (peace be upon him): regarding His saying: **لَئِنْ أَخَّرْنا عَنْهُمُ الْعَذابَ إِلى أُمَّةٍ مَعْدُودَةٍ** "And if We defer their punishment until a reckoned community" (11:8), he said: "The punishment (العذاب) is the rising of the *Qa’im* (peace be upon him)—and the reckoned community (الأمة المعدودة) are the people of Badr and his companions."

**I say:** al-Kulayni narrated this meaning in *al-Kafi*, and al-Qummi and al-‘Ayyashi in their two *Tafsirs* from ‘Ali, al-Baqir, and al-Sadiq (peace be upon them).

In *al-Majma‘*: It was said: "The reckoned community are the companions of the *Mahdi*—three hundred and ten-odd men, like the number of the people of Badr—they gather in a single hour just as the autumn clouds gather—." He said: "And it is what is narrated from Abu Ja‘far and Abu ‘Abdullah (peace be upon them)."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: regarding His saying: **إِلَّا الَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ** "excepting those who are patient and do righteous deeds" (11:11), he said: "He said: 'They were patient in hardship and did righteous deeds in ease.'"

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: regarding His saying: **مَنْ كانَ يُرِيدُ الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا** "As for those who desire the life of this world" (11:15): al-Bayhaqi in *al-Shu‘ab* narrated from Anas, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'When the Day of Resurrection comes, my nation will become three groups: a group who worshiped Allah sincerely, a group who worshiped Allah out of ostentation (رياءً), and a group who worshiped Allah to attain the world thereby. He will say to the one who used to worship Allah for the world: "By My might and My majesty, what did you intend by My worship?" He will say: "The world." He will say: "No doubt, what you gathered shall not benefit you—nor shall you return to it; take him to the Fire." And He will say to the one who worshiped Allah out of ostentation: "By My might and My majesty, what did you intend by My worship?" He said: "Ostentation." He will say: "Your worship by which you used to show off—nothing of it ascended to Me, and it shall not benefit you today—take him to the Fire—."'"

"'And He will say to the one who used to worship Allah sincerely: "By My might and My majesty, what did you intend by My worship?" He will say: "By Your might and Your majesty, You are more knowledgeable of it than I—I used to worship You for Your sake and for Your abode—." He said: "My servant has spoken the truth; take him to Paradise."'"
